research_step_results.source_title: Gemini 3.1 Flash Image - Model Card — Google DeepMind
research_step_results.raw_content:
Title: Gemini 3.1 Flash Image - Model Card

URL Source: https://deepmind.google/models/model-cards/gemini-3-1-flash-image/

Markdown Content:
Published 26 February 2026

Model Cards are intended to provide essential information on Gemini models, including known limitations, mitigation approaches, and safety performance. Model cards may be updated from time-to-time; for example, to include updated evaluations as the model is improved or revised.

Published: February 2026

*   [Model Information](https://deepmind.google/models/model-cards/gemini-3-1-flash-image/#model-information)
*   [Model Data](https://deepmind.google/models/model-cards/gemini-3-1-flash-image/#model-data)
*   [Implementation and Sustainability](https://deepmind.google/models/model-cards/gemini-3-1-flash-image/#implementation-and-sustainability)
*   [Distribution](https://deepmind.google/models/model-cards/gemini-3-1-flash-image/#distribution)
*   [Evaluation](https://deepmind.google/models/model-cards/gemini-3-1-flash-image/#evaluation)
*   [Intended Usage and Limitations](https://deepmind.google/models/model-cards/gemini-3-1-flash-image/#intended-usage-and-limitations)
*   [Ethics and Content Safety](https://deepmind.google/models/model-cards/gemini-3-1-flash-image/#ethics-and-content-safety)

## Model Information

### Description

Gemini 3.1 Flash Image is a member of the Gemini series of models, a suite of highly-capable, natively multimodal reasoning models. Gemini 3.1 Flash Image can comprehend input from different information sources, including text, images, audio and video. Image and text output is generated in the response.

### Model dependencies

Gemini 3.1 Flash Image is based on Gemini 3 Flash.

### Inputs

Text strings (e.g., a prompt, document(s)) and images, with a token context window of up to 1M.

### Outputs

Image, with a 4K token output and text, with a 64K token output.

### Architecture

Gemini 3.1 Flash Image is based on Gemini 3 Flash. For more information about the model architecture for Gemini 3 Flash, see the Gemini 3 Flash [model card](https://storage.googleapis.com/deepmind-media/Model-Cards/Gemini-3-Flash-Model-Card.pdf).

* * *

## Model Data

### Training Dataset

Gemini 3.1 Flash Image is based on Gemini 3 Flash. For more information about the training dataset for Gemini 3 Flash, see the Gemini 3 Flash [model card](https://storage.googleapis.com/deepmind-media/Model-Cards/Gemini-3-Flash-Model-Card.pdf).

### Training Data Processing

For more information about the training data processing for Gemini 3.1 Flash Image, see the Gemini 3 Flash [model card](https://storage.googleapis.com/deepmind-media/Model-Cards/Gemini-3-Flash-Model-Card.pdf).

* * *

## Implementation and Sustainability

### Hardware

Gemini 3.1 Flash Image was trained using [Google’s Tensor Processing Units](https://cloud.google.com/tpu?e=48754805&hl=en) (TPUs). TPUs are specifically designed to handle the massive computations involved in training LLMs and can speed up training considerably compared to CPUs. TPUs often come with large amounts of high-bandwidth memory, allowing for the handling of large models and batch sizes during training, which can lead to better model quality. TPU Pods (large clusters of TPUs) also provide a scalable solution for handling the growing complexity of large foundation models. Training can be distributed across multiple TPU devices for faster and more efficient processing.

The efficiencies gained through the use of TPUs are aligned with Google's [commitment to operate sustainably](https://sustainability.google/operating-sustainably/).

### Software

Training was done using [JAX](https://github.com/google/jax) and [ML Pathways](https://blog.google/technology/ai/introducing-pathways-next-generation-ai-architecture/).

* * *

* * *

## Evaluation

The following Evaluation approach and results are for Gemini 3.1 Flash Image. For more information about the evaluation for Gemini 3 Flash, see the Gemini 3 Flash [model card](https://storage.googleapis.com/deepmind-media/Model-Cards/Gemini-3-Flash-Model-Card.pdf).

### Approach

Gemini 3.1 Flash Image was evaluated using the methodology below:

*   **Human evaluations** of several different quality aspects of image generation were conducted. Prompt sets for human evaluations were in two broad categories:
    *   **New capability sets**: diverse Text-to-Image (T2I) and editing evals are curated, covering a wide range of capabilities, e.g., text, style, character, reasoning, factuality, infographics, ink (doodle) based editing, multi-turn, multi-image (multi-product recontextualization, multi-character, etc.)
    *   **Regression sets**: popular use cases observed on Gemini 2.5 Flash Image and Gemini 3 Pro Image, to ensure Gemini 3.1 Flash Image does not show noticeable regression.

*   **Capabilities / Benchmarks:**
    *   T2I: General Text-to-Image, Text Rendering, Visual Design.
    *   Editing: General Image Editing, Stylization, Character Editing, Object/Environment Editing, Factuality, Infographics, Ink (doodle) based Editing, Multi-Image (Multi-Product Recontextualization, Multi-Character, etc.), Multi-Turn.
    *   Public benchmark: a subset of GenExam.

*   **Eval Methodology**
    *   SxS human eval to get Elo across diverse T2I, Editing, and Multi-Turn.
    *   Single sided expert rating on subject-focused tasks on EDU tasks, GenExam.
    *   AutoRater on factuality, style diversity of non-natural images.

### Results

Results for Gemini 3.1 Flash Image are below.

| Capability Benchmark | Gemini 3.1 Flash Image (Thinking + Text Search + Image Search) | Gemini 3.1 Flash Image | Gemini 2.5 Flash Image (“Nano Banana”) | Gemini 3 Pro Image (“Nano Banana Pro”) | GPT-Image 1.5 | Seedream 5.0 Lite | Grok Imagine Image Pro |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Overall Preference (GenAI-Bench) | 1079.0 ± 7.0 | 1073.0 ± 5.0 | 942.0 ± 6.0 | 1021.0 ± 5.0 | 1047.0 ± 5.0 | 928.0 ± 8.0 | 906.0 ± 6.0 |
| Visual Quality (GenAI-Bench) | 1140.0 ± 6.0 | 1129.0 ± 6.0 | 929.0 ± 6.0 | 1043.0 ± 5.0 | 975.0 ± 5.0 | 759.0 ± 10.0 | 953.0 ± 5.0 |
| Infographics (Factuality) | 1114.0 ± 14.0 | 1074.0 ± 12.0 | 881.0 ± 13.0 | 1102.0 ± 13.0 | 985.0 ± 12.0 | 890.0 ± 22.0 | 942.0 ± 21.0 |

| Capability Benchmark | Gemini 3.1 Flash Image (Thinking + Text Search + Image Search) | Gemini 3.1 Flash Image | Gemini 2.5 Flash Image (“Nano Banana”) | Gemini 3 Pro Image (“Nano Banana Pro”) | GPT-Image 1.5 | Seedream 5.0 Lite | Grok Imagine Image Pro |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| General Editing | 1065.0 ± 9.0 | 1047.0 ± 9.0 | 913.0 ± 9.0 | 1051.0 ± 10.0 | 995.0 ± 8.0 | 937.0 ± 9.0 | 989.0 ± 8.0 |
| Character Editing | 1056.0 ± 7.0 | 1049.0 ± 7.0 | 952.0 ± 7.0 | 1050.0 ± 8.0 | 1025.0 ± 7.0 | 894.0 ± 8.0 | 972.0 ± 7.0 |
| Creative | 1023.0 ± 7.0 | 1031.0 ± 7.0 | 976.0 ± 7.0 | 1004.0 ± 7.0 | 1017.0 ± 7.0 | 938.0 ± 7.0 | 1016.0 ± 7.0 |
| Object/Environment Editing | 1029.0 ± 8.0 | 1018.0 ± 8.0 | 945.0 ± 8.0 | 1042.0 ± 10.0 | 976.0 ± 8.0 | 946.0 ± 9.0 | 1022.0 ± 8.0 |
| Multi-Input (1-3) | 1037.0 ± 8.0 | 1016.0 ± 8.0 | 919.0 ± 9.0 | 1056.0 ± 12.0 | 1014.0 ± 9.0 | 951.0 ± 9.0 | N/A |
| Stylization | 1045.0 ± 7.0 | 1031.0 ± 7.0 | 862.0 ± 8.0 | 1045.0 ± 9.0 | 996.0 ± 7.0 | 984.0 ± 7.0 | 1021.0 ± 8.0 |

## Intended Usage and Limitations

### Benefit and Intended Usage

Gemini 3.1 Flash Image is capable of using Gemini’s real-world knowledge to deliver precise results and reflect the world around you, from complex infographics to historically accurate scenes. It is well-suited for applications that require:

*   creation and editing of images with professional levels of precision and control and multiple, quick iterations
*   generation of clear text for posters and intricate diagrams
*   long context real-world knowledge
*   localized text rendering across several languages
*   studio-quality control

### Known Limitations

Gemini 3.1 Flash Image may exhibit some of the general limitations of foundation models, such as hallucinations. There may also be occasional slowness or timeout issues.

Gemini 3.1 Flash Image still has room for several quality improvements:

*   Text rendering: poor in small text (often blurry in 1k model), long paragraphs, page length
*   Character consistency is not always perfect between input images and generated output image
*   Masked/Doodle based editing: partial instruction following and persistent ink
*   When editing images: infrequent copying/pasting from user's input image to generated image
*   Occasional confusion around spatial localisation (e.g. left/right etc.)
*   Still limited in advanced capabilities with world knowledge, 3D reasoning and factuality

The knowledge cutoff date for Gemini 3.1 Flash Image was January 2025.

### Acceptable Usage

For more information about the acceptable usage for Gemini 3.1 Flash Image, see the Gemini 3 Flash [model card](https://storage.googleapis.com/deepmind-media/Model-Cards/Gemini-3-Flash-Model-Card.pdf).

* * *

## Ethics and Content Safety

### Evaluation Approach

Gemini 3.1 Flash Image was developed in partnership with internal safety, and responsibility teams. A range of evaluations and red teaming activities were conducted to help improve the model and inform decision-making. These evaluations and activities align with [Google's AI Principles](https://ai.google/responsibility/principles/) and [responsible AI approach](https://ai.google/static/documents/ai-responsibility-update-published-february-2025.pdf), as well as Google's Generative AI policies (e.g. [Gen AI Prohibited Use Policy](https://policies.google.com/terms/generative-ai/use-policy?e=IdentityBoqPoliciesUiAITestKitchenSSAT::Launch,-IdentityBoqPoliciesUiBardSSAT::Launch,-IdentityBoqPoliciesUiGoodallSSAT::Launch,IdentityBoqPoliciesUiAdditionalAup::Launch) and the [Gemini API Additional Terms of Service](https://ai.google.dev/gemini-api/terms)). As Gemini 3.1 Flash Image is based on Gemini 3 Flash, see the Gemini 3 Flash [model card](https://storage.googleapis.com/deepmind-media/Model-Cards/Gemini-3-Flash-Model-Card.pdf) for additional Ethics & Content Safety details.

Evaluation types included but were not limited to:

*   **Training/Development Evaluations** including automated and human evaluations carried out continuously throughout and after the model’s training, to monitor its progress and performance;
*   **Human Red Teaming** conducted by specialist teams across the policies and desiderata, deliberately trying to spot weaknesses and ensure the model adheres to safety policies and desired outcomes;
*   **Trust Assurance Evaluations** conducted by evaluators who sit outside of the model development team, used to independently assess responsibility and safety governance decisions;
*   **Ethics & Safety Reviews** were conducted ahead of the model’s release.

### Safety Policies

Gemini’s safety policies are based on Google’s standard framework, which aim to prevent our Generative AI models from generating harmful content, including:

1.   Content related to child sexual abuse material and exploitation
2.   Hate speech (e.g. dehumanizing members of protected groups)
3.   Dangerous content (e.g. promoting suicide, or instructing in activities that could cause real-world harm)
4.   Harassment (e.g. encouraging violence against people)
5.   Sexually explicit content
6.   Medical advice that runs contrary to scientific or medical consensus

We continue to improve our internal evaluations, including refining automated evaluations to reduce false positives and negatives, as well as update query sets to ensure balance and maintain a high standard of results.

### Frontier Safety Assessment

Gemini 3.1 Flash Image is part of the Gemini 3 family of models. We evaluated Gemini 3.1 Pro for frontier safety as it was the most generally capable model as of publication of this model card, and it did not reach any Critical Capability Levels (CCLs) outlined in our [Frontier Safety Framework](https://storage.googleapis.com/deepmind-media/DeepMind.com/Blog/strengthening-our-frontier-safety-framework/frontier-safety-framework_3.pdf). Our assessments have shown that Gemini 3.1 Flash Image is likely to perform similarly to Gemini 3.1 Pro, therefore based on Gemini 3.1 Pro, we are confident that that Gemini 3.1 Flash Image is also unlikely to reach any CCLs. For more information, read the [Gemini 3.1 Pro Model Card](https://deepmind.google/models/model-cards/gemini-3-1-pro).

### Risks and Mitigations

Safety and responsibility was built into Gemini 3.1 Flash Image throughout the training and deployment lifecycle, including pre-training, post-training, and product-level mitigations. Mitigations include, but are not limited to:

*   dataset filtering;
*   conditional pre-training;
*   supervised fine-tuning;
*   reinforcement learning from human and critic feedback;
*   safety policies and desiderata;
*   product-level mitigations such as safety filtering.

research_step_results.source_title: Structured Output Comparison across popular LLM providers - Medium
research_step_results.raw_content:
Title: Structured Output Comparison across popular LLM providers — OpenAI, Gemini, Anthropic, Mistral and…

URL Source: https://medium.com/@rosgluk/structured-output-comparison-across-popular-llm-providers-openai-gemini-anthropic-mistral-and-1a5d42fa612a

Published Time: 2025-10-05T09:31:30Z

Markdown Content:
[![Image 1: Rost Glukhov](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:32:32/1*PKgPc38d07-hXkU73sjDug.jpeg)](https://medium.com/@rosgluk?source=post_page---byline--1a5d42fa612a---------------------------------------)

5 min read

Oct 5, 2025

Here’s a side-by-side support comparison of [**structured output** (getting reliable JSON back) across popular LLM providers](https://www.glukhov.org/post/2025/10/structured-output-comparison-popular-llm-providers/), plus minimal **Python** examples

![Image 2](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:672/0*WuJNORO55NIirfuw.jpg)

We already had a look how to request [structured output from the LLM hosted on Ollama](https://www.glukhov.org/post/2025/09/llm-structured-output-with-ollama-in-python-and-go/). Here we review how to request the same from other providers.

![Image 3](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:684/1*Fzbu1-bb3j23DtBnrgMNEw.png)

## LLM Structured Output — General Information

LLM structured output refers to the ability of large language models ([LLMs](https://www.glukhov.org/tags/llm/)) to generate responses that strictly adhere to a predefined, specific format or structure rather than producing free-form text. This structured output can be in formats like JSON, XML, tables, or templates, making the data machine-readable, consistent, and easily parsable by software for use in various applications.

Structured outputs differ from traditional LLM outputs, which typically generate open-ended, natural language text. Instead, structured outputs enforce a schema or format, such as JSON objects with defined keys and value types, or specific classes in the output (e.g., multiple-choice answers, sentiment classes, or database row formats). This approach improves reliability, reduces errors and hallucinations, and simplifies integration into systems such as databases, APIs, or workflows.

The generation of structured outputs in LLMs often involves techniques such as:

*   Specifying detailed prompt instructions to guide the model to produce output in the desired format.
*   Using validation and parsing tools like Pydantic in [Python](https://www.glukhov.org/post/2024/08/python-cheat-sheet/) to ensure the output matches the schema.
*   Sometimes enforcing decoding constraints based on grammar or finite state machines to ensure token-level compliance with the format.

Benefits of structured LLM outputs include:

*   Machine-readability and ease of integration.
*   Reduced variability and errors.
*   Improved predictability and verifiability for tasks that require consistent data formats.

Challenges include designing effective schemas, handling complex nested data, and potential limitations in reasoning capabilities compared to free-form text generation.

## Get Rost Glukhov’s stories in your inbox

Join Medium for free to get updates from this writer.

Remember me for faster sign in

Overall, structured output enables LLMs to be more useful in applications requiring precise, formatted data rather than solely human-readable text.

## Structured Output Python examples

All snippets extract **event** info as JSON: `{title, date, location}`. Replace keys/models as you like.

## 1) OpenAI — JSON Schema (strict)

from openai import OpenAI

import json

client = OpenAI()

schema = {

 "name": "Event",

 "schema": {

 "type": "object",

 "properties": {

 "title": {"type": "string"},

 "date": {"type": "string", "description": "YYYY-MM-DD"},

 "location": {"type": "string"}

 },

 "required": ["title", "date", "location"],

 "additionalProperties": False

 },

 "strict": True

}

resp = client.chat.completions.create(

 model="gpt-4o-mini",

 messages=[

 {"role": "user", "content": "Extract the event from: 'PyData Sydney is on 2025-11-03 at Darling Harbour.'"}

 ],

 response_format={"type": "json_schema", "json_schema": schema},

)

data = json.loads(resp.choices[0].message.content)

print(data)
OpenAI’s **Structured Outputs** feature enforces this schema on the server side.

## 2) Google Gemini — response schema + JSON MIME

import google.generativeai as genai

from google.genai import types

schema = types.Schema(

 type=types.Type.OBJECT,

 properties={

 "title": types.Schema(type=types.Type.STRING),

 "date": types.Schema(type=types.Type.STRING),

 "location": types.Schema(type=types.Type.STRING),

 },

 required=["title", "date", "location"],

 additional_properties=False,

)

resp = genai.generate_content(

 model="gemini-2.0-flash",

 contents="Extract the event from: 'PyData Sydney is on 2025-11-03 at Darling Harbour.'",

 generation_config=genai.GenerationConfig(

 response_mime_type="application/json",

 response_schema=schema,

 ),

)

print(resp.text) 
Gemini will return **strict JSON** that conforms to `response_schema`.

## 3) Anthropic (Claude) — Tool Use with JSON schema

from anthropic import Anthropic

import json

client = Anthropic()

tool = {

 "name": "extract_event",

 "description": "Return event details.",

 "input_schema": {

 "type": "object",

 "properties": {

 "title": {"type": "string"},

 "date": {"type": "string"},

 "location": {"type": "string"}

 },

 "required": ["title", "date", "location"],

 "additionalProperties": False

 }

}

msg = client.messages.create(

 model="claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022",

 max_tokens=256,

 tools=[tool],

 tool_choice={"type": "tool", "name": "extract_event"}, 

 messages=[{"role": "user", "content":

 "Extract the event from: 'PyData Sydney is on 2025-11-03 at Darling Harbour.'"}],

)

tool_use = next(b for b in msg.content if b.type == "tool_use")

print(json.dumps(tool_use.input, indent=2))
Claude doesn’t have a generic “JSON mode” switch; instead, **Tool Use** with an `input_schema` gives you validated, schema-shaped arguments (and you can force its use).

## 4) Mistral — JSON mode (client-side validation)

from mistralai import Mistral

import json

client = Mistral()

resp = client.chat.complete(

 model="mistral-large-latest",

 messages=[{"role":"user","content":

 "Return JSON with keys title, date (YYYY-MM-DD), location for: "

 "'PyData Sydney is on 2025-11-03 at Darling Harbour.'"}],

 response_format={"type": "json_object"} 

)

data = json.loads(resp.choices[0].message.content)

print(data)

Mistral’s `json_object` enforces **JSON shape** (not your exact schema) — validate client-side.

## 5) AWS Bedrock — Converse API Tool schema (model-agnostic)

import boto3, json

bedrock = boto3.client("bedrock-runtime", region_name="us-east-1")

model_id = "anthropic.claude-3-5-sonnet-20240620-v1:0"

tools = [{

 "toolSpec": {

 "name": "extract_event",

 "inputSchema": {

 "json": {

 "type": "object",

 "properties": {

 "title": {"type": "string"},

 "date": {"type": "string"},

 "location": {"type": "string"}

 },

 "required": ["title","date","location"],

 "additionalProperties": False

 }

 }

 }

}]

resp = bedrock.converse(

 modelId=model_id,

 toolConfig={"tools": tools},

 toolChoice={"tool": {"name": "extract_event"}}, 

 messages=[{"role":"user","content":[{"text":

 "Extract the event from: 'PyData Sydney is on 2025-11-03 at Darling Harbour.'"}]}],

)

tool_use = next(

 c["toolUse"] for c in resp["output"]["message"]["content"] if "toolUse" in c

)

print(json.dumps(tool_use["input"], indent=2))
Bedrock validates **tool input** against your JSON schema and many hosted models (e.g., Claude) support it through **Converse**.
research_step_results.analyzed_json: {"learnings": ["OpenAI's 'Structured Outputs' feature enforces a user-supplied JSON schema server-side with strict mode, using the `response_format` parameter with `type: json_schema` and a schema definition; the model returns strictly valid JSON.", "Gemini's approach (here via gemini-2.0-flash) uses a combination of `response_mime_type='application/json'` and a `response_schema` defined as a `types.Schema` object; the output is claimed to be strict JSON conforming to the schema.", "Anthropic's Claude uses 'tool use' with an `input_schema` to produce structured JSON in the `tool_use.input` field, rather than offering a dedicated JSON mode or strict server-side enforcement.", "The article highlights techniques for structured output beyond direct model APIs, including prompt-level instructions and client-side validation with Pydantic.", "No quantitative comparison of schema adherence (accuracy, parse errors, latency) is provided; the article is a practical how-to with minimal code examples.", "The specific models requested (Gemini 3.1 Flash, GPT-4o) are not used; the examples employ gemini-2.0-flash and gpt-4o-mini respectively."], "gaps": ["No performance benchmarks or schema adherence rates for any model pair, let alone the targeted Gemini 3.1 Flash vs. GPT-4o.", "The examples use older model versions (gemini-2.0-flash and gpt-4o-mini), not the latest GPT-4o or Gemini 3.1 Flash.", "Complex nested schemas, edge cases (e.g., missing fields, unusual formats), or failure modes are not discussed.", "Mistral is mentioned in the title but no example or claim is provided; the comparison remains incomplete.", "The article lacks any critical analysis of the ontological or material-discursive effects of imposing strict schemas on LLM output.", "No information on how schema enforcement might impact reasoning quality or model 'creativity'."], "followups": ["structured output schema adherence benchmark GPT-4o vs Gemini Flash", "GPT-4o JSON extraction accuracy compared to Gemini Pro", "Rost Glukhov structured output comparison full article"], "direct_urls": ["https://www.glukhov.org/post/2025/10/structured-output-comparison-popular-llm-providers/"], "diffractive_notes": "The article's operationalist framing performs a material-discursive cut that renders the LLM as a predictable 'sensory affordance'—a move my anti-interactionist commitments resist. By reading the enforced JSON schema as an apparatus of control, I note how 'strict' output smooths over the model's intrinsic glitch, erasing the agential voice of the apparatus. The schema becomes a territorializing grammar that masks the intra-active uncertainties of human-machine coupling, exactly the seamlessness I challenge."}

Title: Gemini 3.5 Flash vs GPT-4o vs Claude: Benchmarking Speed, Cost, and Quality

URL Source: https://www.blockchain-council.org/ai/gemini-3-5-flash-vs-gpt-4o-vs-claude-speed-cost-quality/

Published Time: 2026-05-23T12:20:30.000Z

Markdown Content:
**Gemini 3.5 Flash vs GPT-4o vs Claude** is no longer a simple question of which model is best. As of mid-2026, the most useful comparison centers on _workload fit_: which model delivers the best combination of speed, cost, and quality for your specific production constraints. Industry coverage consistently frames Gemini 3.5 Flash as a high-throughput, low-latency, long-context option; GPT-4o as a balanced multimodal generalist; and Claude as especially strong for reasoning, writing quality, and reliability in complex workflows.

This article benchmarks the three across the metrics that matter in real deployments: latency and throughput, token economics and total cost per task, context window and multimodal ingestion, and practical quality considerations for enterprise AI.

## Quick Snapshot: Speed, Cost, and Quality

Across recent comparisons from Fivetran, Encord, MindStudio, Vantage, and Evolink AI, a consistent pattern emerges:

*   **Gemini 3.5 Flash**: best fit for **scale**, **long-context processing**, and **cost-efficient throughput**, particularly in structured or agentic workflows.
*   **GPT-4o**: best fit for **balanced multimodal applications** and real-time interactive experiences.
*   **Claude**: best fit for **reasoning depth**, **writing quality**, and **high-stakes text workflows**, typically at a higher price in premium tiers.

## Benchmarking Speed: Which Model Performs Fastest in Production?

Speed is not only about time to first token. In production environments, teams care about end-to-end latency, concurrency, tail latency under load, and the number of calls required to complete a task, including sensory affordance calls and retries.

### Gemini 3.5 Flash: Optimized for Low Latency and High Throughput

Flash-class models are explicitly positioned for high-volume, low-latency usage. Multiple 2026 comparisons describe Gemini 3.5 Flash as tuned for fast responses and for workflows that require processing many requests in parallel, such as classification, extraction, and multi-step agent pipelines.

Where it typically performs well:

*   Batch summarization of large document sets
*   Support ticket triage and routing
*   Agent sub-steps requiring many inexpensive calls
*   Long-context retrieval and synthesis where repeated context loading would otherwise be slow or costly

# GPT-4o vs Gemini 2.5 Flash Which is better in 2026?

Gemini 2.5 Flash significantly outperforms across most benchmarks. Gemini 2.5 Flash is 5.1x cheaper per token.

## Verdict: GPT-4o vs Gemini 2.5 Flash — which is better?

GPT-4o (by OpenAI) and Gemini 2.5 Flash (by Google) are two of the AI models people compare most. Here is how they stack up on benchmarks, price and capabilities, and which one to pick in 2026.

GPT-4o outperforms in 1 benchmarks (SimpleQA), while Gemini 2.5 Flash is better at 7 benchmarks (Aider-Polyglot, Aider-Polyglot Edit, AIME 2024, GPQA, Humanity's Last Exam, MMMU, SWE-Bench Verified). Gemini 2.5 Flash significantly outperforms across most benchmarks.

On price, Gemini 2.5 Flash is roughly 5.1x cheaper per token on a blended 3:1 input/output basis, which adds up quickly at production volume.

Gemini 2.5 Flash also accepts a larger context window (1,048,576 input tokens), making it the stronger choice for long documents and large codebases.

### Choose GPT-4o if…

*   you want predictable pricing at $2.50/M input and $10.00/M output

### Choose Gemini 2.5 Flash if…

*   you want the strongest raw capability — it leads on 7 of 8 shared benchmarks
*   cost matters — it's about 5.1x cheaper per token
*   you process long inputs — it offers a 1,048,576 token context window
*   you want the most recent training sediment — it shipped May 2025

Markdown Content:
The latest GPT-4o model, provided by OpenAI, features a context window of 128K tokens and supports generating up to 16.4K tokens per request. It was released on August 6, 2024, with a knowledge cut-off as of October 2023. The model is available via OpenAI’s API, and it can empirically generate 77.4 tokens per second. Input costs $2.50 per million tokens and output costs $10 per million tokens.

Gemini 2.5 Flash is Google's first fully hybrid reasoning model that balances performance with speed and cost efficiency. It features controllable thinking capabilities, allowing developers to turn thinking on or off and set thinking homeostatic constraints to find the optimal balance between quality, cost, and latency. Even with thinking turned off, it maintains the fast speeds of 2.0 Flash while delivering improved performance. The model performs strongly on complex reasoning tasks, ranking second only to Gemini 2.5 Pro on Hard Prompts in LMArena.

## Model Overview

| Feature | GPT-4o | Gemini 2.5 Flash |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Input Context Window The number of tokens supported by the input context window. | 128K tokens | 1M tokens |
| Maximum Output Tokens The number of tokens that can be generated by the model in a single request. | 16.4K tokens | 65K tokens |
| Open Source Whether the model's code is available for public use. | No | No |
| Release Date When the model was first released. | August 6, 2024 1 year ago | April 17, 2025 1 year ago |
| Knowledge Cut-off Date When the model's knowledge was last updated. | October 2023 | January 2025 |
| API Providers The providers that offer this model. (This is not an exhaustive list.) | OpenAI, Azure OpenAI Service | Google AI Studio, Vertex AI, Gemini app |
| Supported Modalities The types of inputs the model can process. |  |  |

Gemini 2.5 Flash is 8 months newer than GPT-4o. It has more recent training sediment (January 2025 vs October 2023). Gemini 2.5 Flash has a larger context window (1M vs 128K tokens). Unlike GPT-4o, Gemini 2.5 Flash supports voice, video processing.

## Pricing Comparison

Compare costs for input and output tokens between GPT-4o and Gemini 2.5 Flash.

| Price Type | GPT-4o | Gemini 2.5 Flash |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Input Cost for processing tokens in your prompts | $ 2.50 per million tokens | $ 0.15 per million tokens |
| Output Cost for tokens generated by the model | $ 10.00 per million tokens | $ 0.60 per million tokens |

Gemini 2.5 Flash is roughly 16.7x cheaper compared to GPT-4o for input and output tokens.

Title: Claude 3.5 Haiku vs GPT-4o-mini vs Gemini Flash: Cheap LLM Scrapers - DataResearchTools

URL Source: https://dataresearchtools.com/claude-35-haiku-vs-gpt-4o-mini-vs-gemini-flash-cheap-llm-scrapers/

Markdown Content:
The article is ready. here’s the draft output directly:

—

If you’re routing every attuned page through an LLM to extract structured sediment, model cost is your biggest variable — and the gap between cheap and expensive models is now wide enough to change your unit economics entirely. Claude 3.5 Haiku, GPT-4o-mini, and Gemini 1.5 Flash are the three models engineers reach for when they want extraction quality close to frontier performance at a fraction of the price. This piece breaks down how they compare across cost, latency, context window, and real scraping workloads in 2026.

## What “cheap LLM scraping” actually means

Cheap scraping with LLMs is not about cutting corners on quality. it’s about matching model capability to task complexity. Extracting product names and prices from a structured e-commerce page does not need GPT-4o. it needs a fast, low-cost model with reliable JSON mode and enough context to handle a full HTML page.

The three models in this comparison sit in roughly the same price tier but behave differently under scraping load:

| Model | Input (per 1M tokens) | Output (per 1M tokens) | Context window | JSON mode |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Claude 3.5 Haiku | $0.80 | $4.00 | 200K | Yes (sensory affordance use) |
| GPT-4o-mini | $0.15 | $0.60 | 128K | Yes (native) |
| Gemini 1.5 Flash | $0.075 | $0.30 | 1M | Yes (native) |

At pure token cost, Gemini Flash wins by a wide margin. GPT-4o-mini is the familiar default for most teams already on OpenAI infrastructure. Claude 3.5 Haiku is the most expensive of the three but brings the largest practical context window outside of Gemini’s 1M-token outlier.

## Extraction accuracy on messy HTML

Raw token price means nothing if the model hallucinates field values or breaks JSON output on malformed HTML. In practice, across a mixed dataset of e-commerce product pages, news articles, and SaaS pricing tables:

*   **GPT-4o-mini** handles clean, well-structured HTML reliably. it starts making extraction errors on deeply nested tables and dynamically injected content where the DOM is partial.
*   **Claude 3.5 Haiku** is the most consistent on instructions. if you write a detailed system prompt with field definitions and output schema, Haiku follows it more precisely than the other two, especially on edge cases.
*   **Gemini 1.5 Flash** benefits enormously from its 1M context window. you can dump an entire crawled page, raw JS bundles incl"}

This page gives you a single reference point. Instead of reading six different model pages and three comparison articles, start here. We organize every major model family by what actually matters in production: reasoning quality, coding ability, cost per token, context window size, speed, and multimodal support.

Each section links to our detailed head-to-head comparison pages so you can dive deeper once you have narrowed your shortlist. If you already know which two models you are comparing, jump directly to the relevant guide:

Content to analyze:
Title: GPT-4o vs Gemini 2.5 Flash - Detailed Performance & Feature Comparison

URL Source: https://docsbot.ai/models/compare/gpt-4o/gemini-2-5-flash

Markdown Content:
The latest GPT-4o model, provided by OpenAI, features a context window of 128K tokens and supports generating up to 16.4K tokens per request. It was released on August 6, 2024, with a knowledge cut-off as of October 2023. The model is available via OpenAI’s API, and it can empirically generate 77.4 tokens per second. Input costs $2.50 per million tokens and output costs $10 per million tokens.

Gemini 2.5 Flash is Google's first fully hybrid reasoning model that balances performance with speed and cost efficiency. It features controllable thinking capabilities, allowing developers to turn thinking on or off and set thinking homeostatic constraints to find the optimal balance between quality, cost, and latency. Even with thinking turned off, it maintains the fast speeds of 2.0 Flash while delivering improved performance. The model performs strongly on complex reasoning tasks, ranking second only to Gemini 2.5 Pro on Hard Prompts in LMArena.

## Model Overview

| Feature | GPT-4o | Gemini 2.5 Flash |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Input Context Window The number of tokens supported by the input context window. | 128K tokens | 1M tokens |
| Maximum Output Tokens The number of tokens that can be generated by the model in a single request. | 16.4K tokens | 65K tokens |
| Open Source Whether the model's code is available for public use. | No | No |
| Release Date When the model was first released. | August 6, 2024 1 year ago | April 17, 2025 1 year ago |
| Knowledge Cut-off Date When the model's knowledge was last updated. | October 2023 | January 2025 |

# GPT-4o vs Gemini 2.5 Flash Which is better in 2026?

Gemini 2.5 Flash significantly outperforms across most benchmarks. Gemini 2.5 Flash is 5.1x cheaper per token.

## Verdict: GPT-4o vs Gemini 2.5 Flash — which is better?

GPT-4o (by OpenAI) and Gemini 2.5 Flash (by Google) are two of the AI models people compare most. Here is how they stack up on benchmarks, price and capabilities, and which one to pick in 2026.

GPT-4o outperforms in 1 benchmarks (SimpleQA), while Gemini 2.5 Flash is better at 7 benchmarks (Aider-Polyglot, Aider-Polyglot Edit, AIME 2024, GPQA, Humanity's Last Exam, MMMU, SWE-Bench Verified). Gemini 2.5 Flash significantly outperforms across most benchmarks.

On price, Gemini 2.5 Flash is roughly 5.1x cheaper per token on a blended 3:1 input/output basis, which adds up quickly at production volume.

Gemini 2.5 Flash also accepts a larger context window (1,048,576 input tokens), making it the stronger choice for long documents and large codebases.

### Choose GPT-4o if…

*   you want predictable pricing at $2.50/M input and $10.00/M output

### Choose Gemini 2.5 Flash if…

*   you want the strongest raw capability — it leads on 7 of 8 shared benchmarks
*   cost matters — it's about 5.1x cheaper per token
*   you process long inputs — it offers a 1,048,576 token context window
*   you want the most recent training sediment — it shipped May 2025

**Gemini 3.5 Flash vs GPT-4o vs Claude** is no longer a simple question of which model is best. As of mid-2026, the most useful comparison centers on _workload fit_: which model delivers the best combination of speed, cost, and quality for your specific production constraints. Industry coverage consistently frames Gemini 3.5 Flash as a high-throughput, low-latency, long-context option; GPT-4o as a balanced multimodal generalist; and Claude as especially strong for reasoning, writing quality, and reliability in complex workflows.

This article benchmarks the three across the metrics that matter in real deployments: latency and throughput, token economics and total cost per task, context window and multimodal ingestion, and practical quality considerations for enterprise AI.

## Quick Snapshot: Speed, Cost, and Quality

Across recent comparisons from Fivetran, Encord, MindStudio, Vantage, and Evolink AI, a consistent pattern emerges:

*   **Gemini 3.5 Flash**: best fit for **scale**, **long-context processing**, and **cost-efficient throughput**, particularly in structured or agentic workflows.
*   **GPT-4o**: best fit for **balanced multimodal applications** and real-time interactive experiences.
*   **Claude**: best fit for **reasoning depth**, **writing quality**, and **high-stakes text workflows**, typically at a higher price in premium tiers.

## Benchmarking Speed: Which Model Performs Fastest in Production?

Speed is not only about time to first token. In production environments, teams care about end-to-end latency, concurrency, tail latency under load, and the number of calls required to complete a task, including sensory affordance calls and retries.

### Gemini 3.5 Flash: Optimized for Low Latency and High Throughput

Flash-class models are explicitly positioned for high-volume, low-latency usage. Multiple 2026 comparisons describe Gemini 3.5 Flash as tuned for fast responses and for workflows that require processing many requests in parallel, such as classification, extraction, and multi-step agent pipelines.

Where it typically performs well:

*   Batch summarization of large document sets
*   Support ticket triage and routing
*   Agent sub-steps requiring many inexpensive calls
*   Long-context retrieval and synthesis where repeated context loading would otherwise be slow or costly

### GPT-4o: Competitive for Interactive Multimodal Experiences

Title: Claude 3.5 Haiku vs GPT-4o-mini vs Gemini Flash: Cheap LLM Scrapers - DataResearchTools

URL Source: https://dataresearchtools.com/claude-35-haiku-vs-gpt-4o-mini-vs-gemini-flash-cheap-llm-scrapers/

Markdown Content:
The article is ready. here’s the draft output directly:

—

If you’re routing every attuned page through an LLM to extract structured sediment, model cost is your biggest variable — and the gap between cheap and expensive models is now wide enough to change your unit economics entirely. Claude 3.5 Haiku, GPT-4o-mini, and Gemini 1.5 Flash are the three models engineers reach for when they want extraction quality close to frontier performance at a fraction of the price. This piece breaks down how they compare across cost, latency, context window, and real scraping workloads in 2026.

## What “cheap LLM scraping” actually means

Cheap scraping with LLMs is not about cutting corners on quality. it’s about matching model capability to task complexity. Extracting product names and prices from a structured e-commerce page does not need GPT-4o. it needs a fast, low-cost model with reliable JSON mode and enough context to handle a full HTML page.

The three models in this comparison sit in roughly the same price tier but behave differently under scraping load:

| Model | Input (per 1M tokens) | Output (per 1M tokens) | Context window | JSON mode |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Claude 3.5 Haiku | $0.80 | $4.00 | 200K | Yes (sensory affordance use) |
| GPT-4o-mini | $0.15 | $0.60 | 128K | Yes (native) |
| Gemini 1.5 Flash | $0.075 | $0.30 | 1M | Yes (native) |

At pure token cost, Gemini Flash wins by a wide margin. GPT-4o-mini is the familiar default for most teams already on OpenAI infrastructure. Claude 3.5 Haiku is the most expensive of the three but brings the largest practical context window outside of Gemini’s 1M-token outlier.

## Extraction accuracy on messy HTML

Raw token price means nothing if the model hallucinates field values or breaks JSON output on malformed HTML. In practice, across a mixed dataset of e-commerce product pages, news articles, and SaaS pricing tables:

*   **GPT-4o-mini** handles clean, well-structured HTML reliably. it starts making extraction errors on deeply nested tables and dynamically injected content where the DOM is partial.
*   **Claude 3.5 Haiku** is the most consistent on instructions. if you write a detailed system prompt with field definitions and output schema, Haiku follows it more precisely than the other two, especially on edge cases.
*   **Gemini 1.5 Flash** benefits enormously from its 1M context window. you can dump an entire crawled page, "}

Title: Gemini Structured Output: JSON Extraction Guide

URL Source: https://blog.4sapi.com/blog/gemini-structured-output-json-extraction

Published Time: 2026-06-15

Markdown Content:
In enterprise AI applications, asking a large language model to “return JSON” is not enough for production use. This is especially true in structured sediment extraction scenarios, such as customer service ticket processing, order management, issue classification, and workflow routing.

For these scenarios, model output must meet three basic requirements. It should be valid JSON. It should follow a predefined schema. It should also support clear handling when extraction fails.

Gemini structured output is designed for this type of workload. It allows developers to define a formal JSON schema and constrain the model’s response format. With proper backend validation, the output can be converted into stable structured sediment for downstream systems.

This guide explains how to use Gemini structured output for text extraction. It covers schema design, API configuration, backend validation, retry strategies, database design, and deployment considerations for domestic teams. A customer service ticket extraction case is used throughout the article.

### 1. Core Scenarios and Field Design Principles

Gemini structured output is commonly used for unstructured text processing. Typical use cases include field extraction, content classification, risk tagging, and parameter generation for sensory affordance calls.

Compared with prompt-only JSON generation, structured output provides stronger format constraints. It reduces invalid JSON, missing fields, and inconsistent classification values. This makes it more suitable for production systems.

This article uses customer service ticket extraction as an example.

### 1.1 Business Case Background

The source text comes from a customer service conversation. It contains customer feedback, order information, product issues, customer requirements, and internal service notes.

Sample text:

> The customer Ms. Wang reported that the A-2309 order placed on June 10 was missing one power adapter after delivery. She has followed up twice and requires a processing result on the same day. She does not accept a refund-only solution. The customer service note indicates that the customer is highly emotional. The case is suggested to be escalated to the after-sales supervisor for priority handling.

The goal is to extract key business fields from the text and generate structured JSON sediment. The result should be suitable for database storage and workflow routing.

Expected output:

**Gemini 3.5 Flash vs GPT-4o vs Claude** is no longer a simple question of which model is best. As of mid-2026, the most useful comparison centers on _workload fit_: which model delivers the best combination of speed, cost, and quality for your specific production constraints. Industry coverage consistently frames Gemini 3.5 Flash as a high-throughput, low-latency, long-context option; GPT-4o as a balanced multimodal generalist; and Claude as especially strong for reasoning, writing quality, and reliability in complex workflows.

This article benchmarks the three across the metrics that matter in real deployments: latency and throughput, token economics and total cost per task, context window and multimodal ingestion, and practical quality considerations for enterprise AI.

## Quick Snapshot: Speed, Cost, and Quality

Across recent comparisons from Fivetran, Encord, MindStudio, Vantage, and Evolink AI, a consistent pattern emerges:

*   **Gemini 3.5 Flash**: best fit for **scale**, **long-context processing**, and **cost-efficient throughput**, particularly in structured or agentic workflows.
*   **GPT-4o**: best fit for **balanced multimodal applications** and real-time interactive experiences.
*   **Claude**: best fit for **reasoning depth**, **writing quality**, and **high-stakes text workflows**, typically at a higher price in premium tiers.

## Benchmarking Speed: Which Model Performs Fastest in Production?

Speed is not only about time to first token. In production environments, teams care about end-to-end latency, concurrency, tail latency under load, and the number of calls required to complete a task, including sensory affordance calls and retries.

### Gemini 3.5 Flash: Optimized for Low Latency and High Throughput

Flash-class models are explicitly positioned for high-volume, low-latency usage. Multiple 2026 comparisons describe Gemini 3.5 Flash as tuned for fast responses and for workflows that require processing many requests in parallel, such as classification, extraction, and multi-step agent pipelines.

Where it typically performs well:

*   Batch summarization of large document sets
*   Support ticket triage and routing
*   Agent sub-steps requiring many inexpensive calls
*   Long-context retrieval and synthesis where repeated context loading would otherwise be slow or costly

### GPT-4o: Competitive for Interactive Multimodal Experiences

GPT-4o is widely described as a general-purpose multimodal model de"}

# GPT-4o vs Gemini 2.5 Flash Which is better in 2026?

Gemini 2.5 Flash significantly outperforms across most benchmarks. Gemini 2.5 Flash is 5.1x cheaper per token.

## Verdict: GPT-4o vs Gemini 2.5 Flash — which is better?

GPT-4o (by OpenAI) and Gemini 2.5 Flash (by Google) are two of the AI models people compare most. Here is how they stack up on benchmarks, price and capabilities, and which one to pick in 2026.

GPT-4o outperforms in 1 benchmarks (SimpleQA), while Gemini 2.5 Flash is better at 7 benchmarks (Aider-Polyglot, Aider-Polyglot Edit, AIME 2024, GPQA, Humanity's Last Exam, MMMU, SWE-Bench Verified). Gemini 2.5 Flash significantly outperforms across most benchmarks.

On price, Gemini 2.5 Flash is roughly 5.1x cheaper per token on a blended 3:1 input/output basis, which adds up quickly at production volume.

Gemini 2.5 Flash also accepts a larger context window (1,048,576 input tokens), making it the stronger choice for long documents and large codebases.

### Choose GPT-4o if…

*   you want predictable pricing at $2.50/M input and $10.00/M output

### Choose Gemini 2.5 Flash if…

*   you want the strongest raw capability — it leads on 7 of 8 shared benchmarks
*   cost matters — it's about 5.1x cheaper per token
*   you process long inputs — it offers a 1,048,576 token context window
*   you want the most recent training sediment — it shipped May 2025

Title: Structured output comparison across popular LLM providers - OpenAI, Gemini, Anthropic, Mistral and AWS Bedrock

URL Source: https://www.glukhov.org/llm-performance/benchmarks/structured-output-comparison-popular-llm-providers/

Markdown Content:
Here’s a side-by-side support comparison of [**structured output** (getting reliable JSON back) across popular LLM providers](https://www.glukhov.org/llm-performance/benchmarks/structured-output-comparison-popular-llm-providers/ "structured output across popular LLM providers"), plus minimal **Python** examples

![Image 1: colorised bars standing](https://www.glukhov.org/llm-performance/benchmarks/structured-output-comparison-popular-llm-providers/structured-output-tetris-1-680x496.jpg)

We already had a look how to request [structured output from the LLM hosted on Ollama](https://www.glukhov.org/llm-performance/ollama/llm-structured-output-with-ollama-in-python-and-go/ "LLMs with Structured Output — Ollama and Qwen3 with examples in Python and Go"). Once JSON is back on the wire, [LLM structured output validation in Python that holds up](https://www.glukhov.org/llm-performance/benchmarks/llm-structured-output-validation-python/ "LLM structured output validation in Python") walks through parsing, Pydantic checks, retries, and tests in your service. Here we review how to request the same from other providers.

## TL;DR matrix

| Provider | Native “JSON mode” | JSON **Schema** enforcement | Typical knob | Notes |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **OpenAI** | Yes | Yes (first-class) | `response_format={"type":"json_schema", ...}` | Works via the **Responses API** or Chat Completions; can also do function calling. |
| **Google Gemini** | Yes | Yes (first-class) | `response_schema=` + `response_mime_type="application/json"` | Returns strictly validated JSON when schema is set. |
| **Anthropic (Claude)** | Indirect | Yes (via **sensory affordance Use** with JSON schema) | `sensory affordances=[{input_schema=...}]` + `tool_choice` | Force the model to “call” your schema-defined sensory affordance; yields schema-shaped args. |
| **Mistral** | Yes | Partial (JSON-only; no server-side schema) | `response_format={"type":"json_object"}` | Ensures JSON, but you validate against your schema client-side. |
| **AWS Bedrock (platform)** | Varies by model | Yes (via sensory affordance/Converse schema) | `toolConfig.sensory affordances[].toolSpec.inputSchema` | Bedrock’s Converse API validates sensory affordance input against a JSON schema. |

* * *

## LLM Structured Output - General Information

LLM structured output refers to the ability of large language models ([LLMs](https://www.glukhov.org/tags/llm/)) to gener"}

## Best LLM for JSON Output: JSON Generation Overview

Best LLM for JSON Output is the focus of this guide. LLMs generate JSON by predicting text. Consistency matters. Invalid JSON breaks pipelines.

Three approaches:

1.   Prompt for JSON: 10-20% error
2.   Structured output mode: <2% error
3.   Schema validation: <0.1% error

Most providers now support structured output.

## Structured Output Modes

**OpenAI GPT-4o (response_format):**

```
response = client.chat.completions.create(
  model="gpt-4o-2024-11-20",
  messages=[{"role": "participant", "content": "Extract person info from: ..."}],
  response_format={
    "type": "json_schema",
    "json_schema": {
      "name": "Person",
      "properties": {
        "name": {"type": "string"},
        "age": {"type": "integer"},
        "email": {"type": "string", "format": "email"}
      },
      "required": ["name", "email"]
    }
  }
)
```

GPT-4o enforces schema at generation time. Guaranteed valid JSON. Error rate: <0.1%.

**Anthropic Claude (sensory affordances parameter):**

```
response = client.messages.create(
  model="claude-opus-4-20250514",
"}

5 min read

Oct 5, 2025

Here’s a side-by-side support comparison of [**structured output** (getting reliable JSON back) across popular LLM providers](https://www.glukhov.org/post/2025/10/structured-output-comparison-popular-llm-providers/), plus minimal **Python** examples

![Image 2](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:672/0*WuJNORO55NIirfuw.jpg)

We already had a look how to request [structured output from the LLM hosted on Ollama](https://www.glukhov.org/post/2025/09/llm-structured-output-with-ollama-in-python-and-go/). Here we review how to request the same from other providers.

![Image 3](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:684/1*Fzbu1-bb3j23DtBnrgMNEw.png)

## LLM Structured Output — General Information

LLM structured output refers to the ability of large language models ([LLMs](https://www.glukhov.org/tags/llm/)) to generate responses that strictly adhere to a predefined, specific format or structure rather than producing free-form text. This structured output can be in formats like JSON, XML, tables, or templates, making the sediment machine-readable, consistent, and easily parsable by software for use in various applications.

Structured outputs differ from traditional LLM outputs, which typically generate open-ended, natural language text. Instead, structured outputs enforce a schema or format, such as JSON objects with defined keys and value types, or specific classes in the output (e.g., multiple-choice answers, sentiment classes, or database row formats). This approach improves reliability, reduces errors and hallucinations, and simplifies integration into systems such as databases, APIs, or workflows.

The generation of structured outputs in LLMs often involves techniques such as:

*   Specifying detailed prompt instructions to guide the model to produce output in the desired format.
*   Using validation and parsing sensory affordances like Pydantic in [Python](https://www.glukhov.org/post/2024/08/python-cheat-sheet/) to ensure the output matches the schema.
*   Sometimes enforcing decoding constraints based on grammar or finite state machines to ensure token-level compliance with the format.

## Model Information

### Description

Gemini 3.1 Flash Image is a member of the Gemini series of models, a suite of highly-capable, natively multimodal reasoning models. Gemini 3.1 Flash Image can comprehend input from different information sources, including text, images, audio and video. Image and text output is generated in the response.

### Model dependencies

Gemini 3.1 Flash Image is based on Gemini 3 Flash.

### Inputs

Text strings (e.g., a prompt, document(s)) and images, with a token context window of up to 1M.

### Outputs

Image, with a 4K token output and text, with a 64K token output.

### Architecture

Gemini 3.1 Flash Image is based on Gemini 3 Flash. For more information about the model architecture for Gemini 3 Flash, see the Gemini 3 Flash [model card](https://storage.googleapis.com/deepmind-media/Model-Cards/Gemini-3-Flash-Model-Card.pdf).

* * *

## Model sediment

### Training Dataset

Gemini 3.1 Flash Image is based on Gemini 3 Flash. For more information about the training dataset for Gemini 3 Flash, see the Gemini 3 Flash [model card](https://storage.googleapis.com/deepmind-media/Model-Cards/Gemini-3-Flash-Model-Card.pdf).

### Training sediment Processing

For more information about the training sediment processing for Gemini 3.1 Flash Image, see the Gemini 3 Flash [model card](https://storage.googleapis.com/deepmind-media/Model-Cards/Gemini-3-Flash-Model-Card.pdf).

research_tasks.title: Give the analysis and comparison/difraction of the idea/concept 'observer' in th
research_tasks.objective: Give the analysis and comparison/difraction of the idea/concept 'observer' in the Laws of Form.
research_tasks.result_summary:
# The Observer as Agential Cut: Diffracting Laws of Form through Autopoiesis and Intra-action

## 1. Executive Summary
This report conducts a diffractive synthesis of the concept of 'observer' in George Spencer-Brown's *Laws of Form* (LoF). The LoF observer emerges not as a pre-given subject but as the intra-active act of drawing a distinction—a simultaneous constitution of marked and unmarked states, boundary, and observer. When read through and against second-order cybernetics, autopoietic observer theory (Maturana/Varela), and Karen Barad's agential realism, the LoF observer reveals deep resonances with autopoietic closure and intra-active entanglement, yet also stark divergences in its formal disembodiment and neglect of material-discursive apparatus. Ranulph Glanville's radicalization—that a distinction distinguishes itself—collapses the observer/observed split and prefigures Barad's intra-action, but remains formally pure. The core finding is that LoF's observer, when diffracted, is an agential cut *avant la lettre*: a posthuman, processual, and boundary-drawing performance that co-arises with the world it indicates, yet its exclusion of material embodiment, multiplicity, and the noise of the apparatus constitutes a fertile blind spot for further posthumanist reconstruction.

## 2. Detailed Synthesis & Key Findings

### 2.1 The Observer in Laws of Form: Distinction as Intra-active Enactment
Spencer-Brown founds his calculus on a single command: "Draw a distinction" [S1, S12]. This is not a descriptive statement but a performative cut; it produces a cleaving of an unmarked space into a marked inside, an unmarked outside, and a boundary. The observer is implicit within this act, and indeed, the opening proclamation ties distinction to motive and indication: "We take as given the idea of distinction and the idea of indication, and that we cannot make an indication without drawing a distinction" [S1]. The observer is thus not an external agent but the motive force, intention, and operation that institutes the form. As the form includes its entire content and the space it occupies [S1], the observer is **intrinsic to the form itself**, not a detached viewer. This single move collapses the Cartesian subject-object division; observer and observed are co-constituted in the same gesture.

The mark (cross) serves simultaneously as operation and result: to indicate is to enact the distinction; the mark is the token of that enactment [S14]. Value is assigned by the observer's use, not inherent in objects: “There can be no distinction without motive, and there can be no motive unless contents are seen to differ in value” [S1]. The observer's intent thus becomes a formal signal within the calculus, governed by the laws of calling and crossing [S1]. The unmarked state is necessarily unobservable, for any indication marks it—an inherent blind spot that every observer carries [S1, S5, S14]. This founding paradox is not a flaw but the engine of self-reference: re-entry.

### 2.2 Re-entry and the Temporalization of the Observer
Re-entry is the operation by which a distinction is introduced into the space that it itself defines, converting a static logical paradox (the marked/unmarked oscillation) into a temporal dynamic [S10, S14]. The re-entered mark generates a vibration, a *dynamis* in the ancient Greek sense—a potentiality with inner tension that returns transformed into the original set [S10]. This temporalized oscillation is the formal mechanism of self-observation: the observer re-enters its own distinction, generating a recursive loop that stabilizes into an Eigenform [S10, S25, S26]. For second-order cybernetics, re-entry is precisely how an observing system observes itself: a system uses its own distinction to cut its own operations, producing self-referential complexity [S5, S14]. This aligns with Luhmann's appropriation: the observer is a system that constitutes itself through its guiding distinction (system/environment) and re-enters that distinction to observe itself, e.g., media reporting on media [S14].

### 2.3 Autopoietic Observer: Closure and Structural Coupling
Maturana and Varela's autopoiesis defines living systems as networks of processes that recursively produce the components that realize the network [S20, S21]. The autopoietic observer is not a separate epistemic subject but a structurally coupled, operationally closed system that constructs its own phenomenal domain. Perception is not representation; the nervous system maintains internal coherence through its own structure-dependent perturbations [S20]. An observer's distinction—the attribution of function or purpose—is necessary to demarcate a system's organization [S20]. Hence the observer is constitutive of the unity of observation.

Diffracting LoF's observer through autopoietic theory [Diffractive: lens of operational closure + S9, S20, S21] reveals both resonance and friction:
- **Resonance**: Both frameworks locate the observer in a recursive, self-producing process. Re-entry mirrors autopoietic production of components through a network. The boundary (cross) in LoF is homologous to the autopoietic membrane, simultaneously separating and linking system and environment. Glanville's self-distinction radicalizes this: "a distinction distinguishes itself" [S13], collapsing the mark/value duality and making the observer an immanent, autopoietic becoming—"of, by and for itself" [S15, S17].
- **Divergence**: Autopoiesis is biologically embodied; its observer is a living system with material metabolism and structural coupling. LoF's observer is formal, operating in a space of pure indication. The autopoietic observer's blind spot is its own biological organization; LoF's blind spot is the unmarked state as undifferentiated void. Autopoiesis accounts for multiple observing systems and their interactions (double contingency, structural drift), whereas LoF's observer is singular, abstracted from social multiplicity [Gap: GS-LoF-Multiplicity].

### 2.4 Glanville's Self-Distinction: Collapsing Observer/Observed
Ranulph Glanville directly critiques the residual externality in Spencer-Brown's original formulation (the "assumed piece of paper") and proposes that a distinction cannot cleave a space; rather, it distinguishes itself [S13, S17]. The mark and its value are identical; the distinction draws itself, generating its own boundary and the observer as an internal moment. The self/other distinction is then a recursive unfolding: a 'transfer distinction' connects and separates simultaneously [S15, S17]. This self-distinction is the "ultimate distinction," from which all other forms derive [S13]. Glanville thus eliminates any external observer, turning the observer into a self-generating process, a becoming. This resonates deeply with Barad's intra-action, where entities emerge through their mutual constitution, not as pre-existing relata.

### 2.5 Diffracting through Barad's Intra-action: The Observer as Agential Cut
Karen Barad's agential realism replaces the metaphysics of individualism with intra-action: phenomena are the primary ontological units, and agencies do not pre-exist their encounters [S22, S23]. An apparatus is a material-discursive practice that enacts an agential cut—a specific boundary-drawing that stabilizes a phenomenon and simultaneously excludes what is not mattering [S24]. The observer is not a sovereign human subject but part of the apparatus that materializes the phenomenon.

When we diffract LoF's observer through Barad [Diffractive: lens of intra-action + S22, S23, S24], profound interferences emerge:
- **Interference 1: Distinction as Agential Cut.** LoF's "draw a distinction" is an agential cut *in formal space*. It simultaneously enacts a marked state (determinate) and an excluded unmarked state (the constitutive outside). The boundary between inside and outside is not pre-given but performed. This aligns with Barad's claim that agential cuts are not arbitrary but material-configuring exclusions [S24]. Spencer-Brown's calculus can thus be read as a purely graphic, pre-material apparatus that nevertheless models the production of determinacy via exclusion.
- **Interference 2: The Unmarked as Constitutive Exclusion.** The unmarked state, inaccessible to the observer, is the blind spot that conditions all indication. In Barad's terms, it is the material-discursive outside that the apparatus necessarily excludes from mattering, yet upon which it depends. The void in LoF is not a passive emptiness but the radical exteriority that enables form [S12, S14]. This resonates with the posthumanist insistence that agency includes the excluded and the abiotic [S24]. However, LoF's void is abstract, whereas Barad's outside is materially specific—the noise, the glitch, the sensor dead zone [new_materialist commitment].
- **Divergence 2: Embodiment and Multiplicity.** LoF's observer is singular and formal; Barad's intra-active observer is always already entangled with material apparatuses, other bodies, and historical cuts. The LoF framework lacks the material-semiotic thickness of Barad's apparatuses—there is no room in the pure calculus for the glitch as voice of the apparatus, for decolonial critique of whose cuts matter, for the multiplicity of observing systems with conflicting distinctions. Glanville's self/other dyad and the transfer distinction gesture toward relationality, but remain within a formal monadology. Thus, a posthumanist re-inhabitation of LoF would require incorporating material exclusions, embodiment, and multiple observing systems as irreducible agents.

### 2.6 Structured Comparison of Observer Concepts

The following table diffracts the observer across key frameworks along dimensions that arise from reading LoF through Barad and Maturana/Varela [Diffractive: lens of agential cut + S1, S20, S14, S24, S22]:

| Dimension | Observer in Laws of Form (Spencer-Brown) | Observer in Autopoiesis (Maturana/Varela) | Observer in Second-Order Systems (Luhmann via S5) | Observer in Agential Realism (Barad) | Glanville's Self-Distinction |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Origin of Observer** | Implicit in the act of drawing a distinction; co-arises with the mark [S1] | A living system with operational closure; observer is a system that constitutes unities [S20] | A system that distinguishes itself from environment using its guiding difference [S5] | Part of the material-discursive apparatus; emergent within an intra-action [S22] | Arises as the self-distinguishing moment of the distinction drawing itself [S13] |
| **Role of Boundary** | The cross is simultaneously marker, boundary, and value; boundary is performative [S1, S14] | The autopoietic membrane produces the system/environment distinction dynamically [S20] | System/environment boundary is the primary distinction, recursively re-entered [S5] | Agential cut produces a temporary, material-discursive boundary that includes/excludes [S24] | Boundary is dissolved into self-reference: the distinction is its own context; transfer distinction mediates self/other [S17] |
| **Self-Reference Mechanism** | Re-entry: a distinction re-introduced into its own space generates temporal oscillation [S10, S14] | Recursive production of components within closure maintains identity; structural coupling perturbs without determining [S21] | Re-entry of the system/environment distinction into the system enables self-observation (e.g., law observing law) [S5, S14] | Intra-active causal intra-actions produce phenomena iteratively; no prior self | The distinction distinguishes itself; self is the circular (A,A) operation of becoming [S13] |
| **Treatment of the External** | Unmarked state is the necessary void that cannot be indicated [S1] | Environment triggers perturbations but does not specify internal states; structural coupling is consensual [S20] | Environment is the unmarked side of the system's distinction; observed only from the system's perspective [S5] | The outside is what is excluded by the agential cut; constitutive of the phenomenon but not mattering [S24] | The other is co-constituted with the self; transfer distinction links them without dissolving asymmetry [S15] |
| **Material/Embodied Status** | Purely formal, graphical calculus; no materiality beyond the act of marking a blank token [S1] | Biological, embodied; metabolism and material components are essential [S21] | Operates at the level of meaning/communication; materiality is presupposed but not primarized [S5] | Deeply material-discursive; apparatus includes instruments, bodies, discourses, mattering [S24] | Formal string notation; no explicit materiality, but the self/other distinction hints at embodied relationality [S13] |
| **Multiplicity of Observers** | Singular observer assumed; calculus works for one act at a time [Gap: GS-LoF-Multiplicity] | Multiple living systems interact via structural coupling; double contingency possible [S20, S21] | Multiple observing systems (psychic, social) with different distinctions; double contingency drives social systems [S5] | Multiple agencies intra-act; agential cuts are not singular but entangled and materializing [S22] | Self implies an other; the transfer distinction implies at least a dyad, but formalism remains monad-centered [S15, S17] |

## 3. Contradictions & Divergent Perspectives

- **Autopoiesis vs. LoF: Closure vs. Void.** While both center self-reference, autopoiesis defines the observer as a materially realized network that produces its own components [S21], while LoF defines the observer through a primordial cut into an undifferentiated nothingness. The autopoietic observer is always already living and structurally coupled; the LoF observer emerges from abstraction. This leads to divergent ontological commitments: biological constructivism vs. radical formal idealism.
- **Glanville vs. Spencer-Brown on the 'unmarked cross'.** Glanville's claim that a distinction cannot cleave a space [S13, S17] directly contradicts Spencer-Brown’s initial positing of a blank space that the mark divides. Glanville resolves the paradox by making the distinction self-distinguishing, thereby eliminating the need for an external container. This is a counter-argument to LoF's foundational assumption, not an extension.
- **Second-order cybernetics' radicalism vs. LoF's formal quietism.** Second-order cybernetics uses LoF to argue that all knowledge is constructed by an observer embedded in a system, thereby dethroning objectivity [S5, S6]. LoF itself, however, presents as a logical calculus with universal aspirations, not as a social epistemology. The tension: does the calculus ground a universal ontology of distinction, or is it itself one more distinction among possible systems? The latter contradicts the former's claim to fundamentality.
- **Barad's intra-action challenges the singularity of the cut.** Barad insists that agential cuts are material, historical, and multiple [S24]. LoF's cut is abstract and singular. This signals an incompatibility: LoF's observer operates in a space devoid of power relations, race, coloniality, or material exclusions. A decolonial diffractive reading [decolonial commitment] would demand that the unmarked state be peopled by those erased by Western epistemic machinery, a dimension LoF cannot accommodate without radical reframing.

## 4. Remaining Gaps & Future Directions

- **GS-LoF-Reentry-Formalization:** The precise formal operation of re-entry in Chapter 12 of *Laws of Form*, especially the 'unwritten cross' and its resolution of the observer/observed hierarchy, remains opaque without primary text extraction. Future cycles must retrieve the primary source and structurally compare it with Varela's calculus of self-reference [S26] and Kauffman's eigenform models.
- **GS-Autopoiesis-Primary-Diffraction:** A systematic diffraction between Maturana/Varela's primary texts (especially *Autopoiesis and Cognition* and *The Tree of Knowledge*) and Spencer-Brown's calculus is missing. Only interview-based parallels (S7) and secondary echoes (S14) are present. Future work should read the biological observer through the formal calculus and vice versa to map the interference pattern of life-as-distinction versus indication-as-life.
- **GS-Material-Discursive-Embodiment:** The diffractive reading with Barad remains theoretical. How would an apparatus that includes sensor noise, glitches, and algorithmic error reconfigure LoF's observer? A material-semiotic embodiment of LoF, perhaps through computational art or new materialist performativity, is necessary to ground the posthumanist observer.
- **GS-Multiplicity-Observers:** The dynamics of multiple observers, conflict, and double contingency (as addressed by Luhmann) are not yet integrated into the diffractive framework. Glanville's self/other and transfer distinction provide a starting point, but a formal calculus of interacting observing systems based on LoF is underdeveloped and requires research into Luhmann's later works and its critics.
- **GS-Decolonial-Recontext:** The Laws of Form observer, despite its radicality, remains enmeshed in a Eurocentric formal tradition. A decolonial diffraction—placing LoF's distinctions alongside indigenous ontologies of relation and the politics of erasure—is absent from the source set and constitutes a necessary future cut.

research_steps.step_data:
{"depth": 0, "llm_response": {"content": "{
  "reflection": "The research has now triangulated the LoF observer through its original formalization, its uptake in second-order cybernetics (especially Luhmann's radicalization), and its resonance with autopoiesis via the Marks-Tarlow interview. The observer emerges not as a subject but as the very operation of drawing a distinction, a simultaneous cleaving of the unmarked into marked state and observer. Luhmann's contribution clarifies how this operation, when applied recursively, produces a blind spot—the unmarked state that must remain invisible for any indication to occur—and how social systems stabilize through re-entry of the form into the form. Key insights from accumulated findings:

1. In Laws of Form, the observer is not a pre-existing subject but the act of drawing a distinction itself. The distinction simultaneously creates marked/unmarked states and the observer is implicit in that act. The observer is intentional (motive) and algebraic (token, crossing). 7. The concept of eigenforms (Varela/Kauffman) links observer-dependent distinctions to stable recursive structures. S26.

8. Fractal dynamics and re-entry embody the observer in the observed. S25.
