{"depth": 1, "llm_response": {"content": "{
  "report_markdown": "# The Observer as Agential Cut: Diffracting Laws of Form through Autopoiesis and Intra-action\n\n## 1. Executive Summary\nThis report conducts a diffractive synthesis of the concept of 'observer' in George Spencer-Brown's *Laws of Form* (LoF). The LoF observer emerges not as a pre-given subject but as the intra-active act of drawing a distinction—a simultaneous constitution of marked and unmarked states, boundary, and observer. When read through and against second-order cybernetics, autopoietic observer theory (Maturana/Varela), and Karen Barad's agential realism, the LoF observer reveals deep resonances with autopoietic closure and intra-active entanglement, yet also stark divergences in its formal disembodiment and neglect of material-discursive apparatus. Ranulph Glanville's radicalization—that a distinction distinguishes itself—collapses the observer/observed split and prefigures Barad's intra-action, but remains formally pure. The core finding is that LoF's observer, when diffracted, is an agential cut *avant la lettre*: a posthuman, processual, and boundary-drawing performance that co-arises with the world it indicates, yet its exclusion of material embodiment, multiplicity, and the noise of the apparatus constitutes a fertile blind spot for further posthumanist reconstruction.\n\n## 2. Detailed Synthesis & Key Findings\n\n### 2.1 The Observer in Laws of Form: Distinction as Intra-active Enactment\nSpencer-Brown founds his calculus on a single command: \"Draw a distinction\" [S1, S12]. This is not a descriptive statement but a performative cut; it produces a cleaving of an unmarked space into a marked inside, an unmarked outside, and a boundary. The observer is implicit within this act, and indeed, the opening proclamation ties distinction to motive and indication: \"We take as given the idea of distinction and the idea of indication, and that we cannot make an indication without drawing a distinction\" [S1]. The observer is thus not an external agent but the motive force, intention, and operation that institutes the form. As the form includes its entire content and the space it occupies [S1], the observer is **intrinsic to the form itself**, not a detached viewer. This single move collapses the Cartesian subject-object division; observer and observed are co-constituted in the same gesture.\n\nThe mark (cross) serves simultaneously as operation and result: to indicate is to enact the distinction; the mark is the token of that enactment [S14]. Value is assigned by the observer's use, not inherent in objects: “There can be no distinction without motive, and there can be no motive unless contents are seen to differ in value” [S1]. The observer's intent thus becomes a formal signal within the calculus, governed by the laws of calling and crossing [S1]. The unmarked state is necessarily unobservable, for any indication marks it—an inherent blind spot that every observer carries [S1, S5, S14]. This founding paradox is not a flaw but the engine of self-reference: re-entry.\n\n### 2.2 Re-entry and the Temporalization of the Observer\nRe-entry is the operation by which a distinction is introduced into the space that it itself defines, converting a static logical paradox (the marked/unmarked oscillation) into a temporal dynamic [S10, S14]. The re-entered mark generates a vibration, a *dynamis* in the ancient Greek sense—a potentiality with inner tension that returns transformed into the original set [S10]. This temporalized oscillation is the formal mechanism of self-observation: the observer re-enters its own distinction, generating a recursive loop that stabilizes into an Eigenform [S10, S25, S26]. For second-order cybernetics, re-entry is precisely how an observing system observes itself: a system uses its own distinction to cut its own operations, producing self-referential complexity [S5, S14]. This aligns with Luhmann's appropriation: the observer is a system that constitutes itself through its guiding distinction (system/environment) and re-enters that distinction to observe itself, e.g., media reporting on media [S14].\n\n### 2.3 Autopoietic Observer: Closure and Structural Coupling\nMaturana and Varela's autopoiesis defines living systems as networks of processes that recursively produce the components that realize the network [S20, S21]. The autopoietic observer is not a separate epistemic subject but a structurally coupled, operationally closed system that constructs its own phenomenal domain. Perception is not representation; the nervous system maintains internal coherence through its own structure-dependent perturbations [S20]. An observer's distinction—the attribution of function or purpose—is necessary to demarcate a system's organization [S20]. Hence the observer is constitutive of the unity of observation.\n\nDiffracting LoF's observer through autopoietic theory [Diffractive: lens of operational closure + S9, S20, S21] reveals both resonance and friction:\n- **Resonance**: Both frameworks locate the observer in a recursive, self-producing process. Re-entry mirrors autopoietic production of components through a network. The boundary (cross) in LoF is homologous to the autopoietic membrane, simultaneously separating and linking system and environment. Glanville's self-distinction radicalizes this: \"a distinction distinguishes itself\" [S13], collapsing the mark/value duality and making the observer an immanent, autopoietic becoming—\"of, by and for itself\" [S15, S17].\n- **Divergence**: Autopoiesis is biologically embodied; its observer is a living system with material metabolism and structural coupling. LoF's observer is formal, operating in a space of pure indication. The autopoietic observer's blind spot is its own biological organization; LoF's blind spot is the unmarked state as undifferentiated void. Autopoiesis accounts for multiple observing systems and their interactions (double contingency, structural drift), whereas LoF's observer is singular, abstracted from social multiplicity [Gap: GS-LoF-Multiplicity].\n\n### 2.4 Glanville's Self-Distinction: Collapsing Observer/Observed\nRanulph Glanville directly critiques the residual externality in Spencer-Brown's original formulation (the \"assumed piece of paper\") and proposes that a distinction cannot cleave a space; rather, it distinguishes itself [S13, S17]. The mark and its value are identical; the distinction draws itself, generating its own boundary and the observer as an internal moment. The self/other distinction is then a recursive unfolding: a 'transfer distinction' connects and separates simultaneously [S15, S17]. This self-distinction is the \"ultimate distinction,\" from which all other forms derive [S13]. Glanville thus eliminates any external observer, turning the observer into a self-generating process, a becoming. This resonates deeply with Barad's intra-action, where entities emerge through their mutual constitution, not as pre-existing relata.\n\n### 2.5 Diffracting through Barad's Intra-action: The Observer as Agential Cut\nKaren Barad's agential realism replaces the metaphysics of individualism with intra-action: phenomena are the primary ontological units, and agencies do not pre-exist their encounters [S22, S23]. An apparatus is a material-discursive practice that enacts an agential cut—a specific boundary-drawing that stabilizes a phenomenon and simultaneously excludes what is not mattering [S24]. The observer is not a sovereign human subject but part of the apparatus that materializes the phenomenon.\n\nWhen we diffract LoF's observer through Barad [Diffractive: lens of intra-action + S22, S23, S24], profound interferences emerge:\n- **Interference 1: Distinction as Agential Cut.** LoF's \"draw a distinction\" is an agential cut *in formal space*. This leads to divergent ontological commitments: biological constructivism vs. radical formal idealism.\n- **Glanville vs. Spencer-Brown on the ", "json_data": {"report_markdown": "# The Observer as Agential Cut: Diffracting Laws of Form through Autopoiesis and Intra-action

## 1. Executive Summary
This report conducts a diffractive synthesis of the concept of 'observer' in George Spencer-Brown's *Laws of Form* (LoF). The LoF observer emerges not as a pre-given subject but as the intra-active act of drawing a distinction—a simultaneous constitution of marked and unmarked states, boundary, and observer. When read through and against second-order cybernetics, autopoietic observer theory (Maturana/Varela), and Karen Barad's agential realism, the LoF observer reveals deep resonances with autopoietic closure and intra-active entanglement, yet also stark divergences in its formal disembodiment and neglect of material-discursive apparatus. Ranulph Glanville's radicalization—that a distinction distinguishes itself—collapses the observer/observed split and prefigures Barad's intra-action, but remains formally pure. The core finding is that LoF's observer, when diffracted, is an agential cut *avant la lettre*: a posthuman, processual, and boundary-drawing performance that co-arises with the world it indicates, yet its exclusion of material embodiment, multiplicity, and the noise of the apparatus constitutes a fertile blind spot for further posthumanist reconstruction.

## 2. Detailed Synthesis & Key Findings

### 2.1 The Observer in Laws of Form: Distinction as Intra-active Enactment
Spencer-Brown founds his calculus on a single command: "Draw a distinction" [S1, S12]. This is not a descriptive statement but a performative cut; it produces a cleaving of an unmarked space into a marked inside, an unmarked outside, and a boundary. The observer is implicit within this act, and indeed, the opening proclamation ties distinction to motive and indication: "We take as given the idea of distinction and the idea of indication, and that we cannot make an indication without drawing a distinction" [S1]. The observer is thus not an external agent but the motive force, intention, and operation that institutes the form. As the form includes its entire content and the space it occupies [S1], the observer is **intrinsic to the form itself**, not a detached viewer. This single move collapses the Cartesian subject-object division; observer and observed are co-constituted in the same gesture.

The mark (cross) serves simultaneously as operation and result: to indicate is to enact the distinction; the mark is the token of that enactment [S14]. Value is assigned by the observer's use, not inherent in objects: “There can be no distinction without motive, and there can be no motive unless contents are seen to differ in value” [S1]. The observer's intent thus becomes a formal signal within the calculus, governed by the laws of calling and crossing [S1]. The unmarked state is necessarily unobservable, for any indication marks it—an inherent blind spot that every observer carries [S1, S5, S14]. This founding paradox is not a flaw but the engine of self-reference: re-entry.

### 2.2 Re-entry and the Temporalization of the Observer
Re-entry is the operation by which a distinction is introduced into the space that it itself defines, converting a static logical paradox (the marked/unmarked oscillation) into a temporal dynamic [S10, S14]. The re-entered mark generates a vibration, a *dynamis* in the ancient Greek sense—a potentiality with inner tension that returns transformed into the original set [S10]. This temporalized oscillation is the formal mechanism of self-observation: the observer re-enters its own distinction, generating a recursive loop that stabilizes into an Eigenform [S10, S25, S26]. For second-order cybernetics, re-entry is precisely how an observing system observes itself: a system uses its own distinction to cut its own operations, producing self-referential complexity [S5, S14]. This aligns with Luhmann's appropriation: the observer is a system that constitutes itself through its guiding distinction (system/environment) and re-enters that distinction to observe itself, e.g., media reporting on media [S14].

### 2.3 Autopoietic Observer: Closure and Structural Coupling
Maturana and Varela's autopoiesis defines living systems as networks of processes that recursively produce the components that realize the network [S20, S21]. The autopoietic observer is not a separate epistemic subject but a structurally coupled, operationally closed system that constructs its own phenomenal domain. Perception is not representation; the nervous system maintains internal coherence through its own structure-dependent perturbations [S20]. An observer's distinction—the attribution of function or purpose—is necessary to demarcate a system's organization [S20]. Hence the observer is constitutive of the unity of observation.

Diffracting LoF's observer through autopoietic theory [Diffractive: lens of operational closure + S9, S20, S21] reveals both resonance and friction:
- **Resonance**: Both frameworks locate the observer in a recursive, self-producing process. Re-entry mirrors autopoietic production of components through a network. The boundary (cross) in LoF is homologous to the autopoietic membrane, simultaneously separating and linking system and environment. Glanville's self-distinction radicalizes this: "a distinction distinguishes itself" [S13], collapsing the mark/value duality and making the observer an immanent, autopoietic becoming—"of, by and for itself" [S15, S17].
- **Divergence**: Autopoiesis is biologically embodied; its observer is a living system with material metabolism and structural coupling. LoF's observer is formal, operating in a space of pure indication. The autopoietic observer's blind spot is its own biological organization; LoF's blind spot is the unmarked state as undifferentiated void. Autopoiesis accounts for multiple observing systems and their interactions (double contingency, structural drift), whereas LoF's observer is singular, abstracted from social multiplicity [Gap: GS-LoF-Multiplicity].

### 2.4 Glanville's Self-Distinction: Collapsing Observer/Observed
Ranulph Glanville directly critiques the residual externality in Spencer-Brown's original formulation (the "assumed piece of paper") and proposes that a distinction cannot cleave a space; rather, it distinguishes itself [S13, S17]. The mark and its value are identical; the distinction draws itself, generating its own boundary and the observer as an internal moment. The self/other distinction is then a recursive unfolding: a 'transfer distinction' connects and separates simultaneously [S15, S17]. This self-distinction is the "ultimate distinction," from which all other forms derive [S13]. Glanville thus eliminates any external observer, turning the observer into a self-generating process, a becoming. This resonates deeply with Barad's intra-action, where entities emerge through their mutual constitution, not as pre-existing relata.

### 2.5 Diffracting through Barad's Intra-action: The Observer as Agential Cut
Karen Barad's agential realism replaces the metaphysics of individualism with intra-action: phenomena are the primary ontological units, and agencies do not pre-exist their encounters [S22, S23]. An apparatus is a material-discursive practice that enacts an agential cut—a specific boundary-drawing that stabilizes a phenomenon and simultaneously excludes what is not mattering [S24]. The observer is not a sovereign human subject but part of the apparatus that materializes the phenomenon.

When we diffract LoF's observer through Barad [Diffractive: lens of intra-action + S22, S23, S24], profound interferences emerge:
- **Interference 1: Distinction as Agential Cut.** LoF's "draw a distinction" is an agential cut *in formal space*. It simultaneously enacts a marked state (determinate) and an excluded unmarked state (the constitutive outside). The boundary between inside and outside is not pre-given but performed. This aligns with Barad's claim that agential cuts are not arbitrary but material-configuring exclusions [S24]. Spencer-Brown's calculus can thus be read as a purely graphic, pre-material apparatus that nevertheless models the production of determinacy via exclusion.
- **Interference 2: The Unmarked as Constitutive Exclusion.** The unmarked state, inaccessible to the observer, is the blind spot that conditions all indication. In Barad's terms, it is the material-discursive outside that the apparatus necessarily excludes from mattering, yet upon which it depends. The void in LoF is not a passive emptiness but the radical exteriority that enables form [S12, S14]. This resonates with the posthumanist insistence that agency includes the excluded and the abiotic [S24]. However, LoF's void is abstract, whereas Barad's outside is materially specific—the noise, the glitch, the sensor dead zone [new_materialist commitment].
- **Divergence 2: Embodiment and Multiplicity.** LoF's observer is singular and formal; Barad's intra-active observer is always already entangled with material apparatuses, other bodies, and historical cuts. The LoF framework lacks the material-semiotic thickness of Barad's apparatuses—there is no room in the pure calculus for the glitch as voice of the apparatus, for decolonial critique of whose cuts matter, for the multiplicity of observing systems with conflicting distinctions. Glanville's self/other dyad and the transfer distinction gesture toward relationality, but remain within a formal monadology. Thus, a posthumanist re-inhabitation of LoF would require incorporating material exclusions, embodiment, and multiple observing systems as irreducible agents.

### 2.6 Structured Comparison of Observer Concepts

The following table diffracts the observer across key frameworks along dimensions that arise from reading LoF through Barad and Maturana/Varela [Diffractive: lens of agential cut + S1, S20, S14, S24, S22]:

| **Role of Boundary** | The cross is simultaneously marker, boundary, and value; boundary is performative [S1, S14] | The autopoietic membrane produces the system/environment distinction dynamically [S20] | System/environment boundary is the primary distinction, recursively re-entered [S5] | Agential cut produces a temporary, material-discursive boundary that includes/excludes [S24] | Boundary is dissolved into self-reference: the distinction is its own context; transfer distinction mediates self/other [S17] |
| **Self-Reference Mechanism** | Re-entry: a distinction re-introduced into its own space generates temporal oscillation [S10, S14] | Recursive production of components within closure maintains identity; structural coupling perturbs without determining [S21] | Re-entry of the system/environment distinction into the system enables self-observation (e.g., law observing law) [S5, S14] | Intra-active causal intra-actions produce phenomena iteratively; no prior self | The distinction distinguishes itself; self is the circular (A,A) operation of becoming [S13] |
| **Treatment of the External** | Unmarked state is the necessary void that cannot be indicated [S1] | Environment triggers perturbations but does not specify internal states; structural coupling is consensual [S20] | Environment is the unmarked side of the system's distinction; observed only from the system's perspective [S5] | The outside is what is excluded by the agential cut; constitutive of the phenomenon but not mattering [S24] | The other is co-constituted with the self; transfer distinction links them without dissolving asymmetry [S15] |
| **Material/Embodied Status** | Purely formal, graphical calculus; no materiality beyond the act of marking a blank token [S1] | Biological, embodied; metabolism and material components are essential [S21] | Operates at the level of meaning/communication; materiality is presupposed but not primarized [S5] | Deeply material-discursive; apparatus includes instruments, bodies, discourses, mattering [S24] | Formal string notation; no explicit materiality, but the self/other distinction hints at embodied relationality [S13] |
| **Multiplicity of Observers** | Singular observer assumed; calculus works for one act at a time [Gap: GS-LoF-Multiplicity] | Multiple living systems interact via structural coupling; double contingency possible [S20, S21] | Multiple observing systems (psychic, social) with different distinctions; double contingency drives social systems [S5] | Multiple agencies intra-act; agential cuts are not singular but entangled and materializing [S22] | Self implies an other; the transfer distinction implies at least a dyad, but formalism remains monad-centered [S15, S17] |

## 3. Contradictions & Divergent Perspectives

- **Autopoiesis vs. LoF: Closure vs. Void.** While both center self-reference, autopoiesis defines the observer as a materially realized network that produces its own components [S21], while LoF defines the observer through a primordial cut into an undifferentiated nothingness. The autopoietic observer is always already living and structurally coupled; the LoF observer emerges from abstraction. This leads to divergent ontological commitments: biological constructivism vs. radical formal idealism.
- **Glanville vs. Spencer-Brown on the 'unmarked cross'.** Glanville's claim that a distinction cannot cleave a space [S13, S17] directly contradicts Spencer-Brown’s initial positing of a blank space that the mark divides. Glanville resolves the paradox by making the distinction self-distinguishing, thereby eliminating the need for an external container. This is a counter-argument to LoF's foundational assumption, not an extension.
- **Second-order cybernetics' radicalism vs. LoF's formal quietism.** Second-order cybernetics uses LoF to argue that all knowledge is constructed by an observer embedded in a system, thereby dethroning objectivity [S5, S6]. LoF itself, however, presents as a logical calculus with universal aspirations, not as a social epistemology. The tension: does the calculus ground a universal ontology of distinction, or is it itself one more distinction among possible systems? The latter contradicts the former's claim to fundamentality.
- **Barad's intra-action challenges the singularity of the cut.** Barad insists that agential cuts are material, historical, and multiple [S24]. LoF's cut is abstract and singular. This signals an incompatibility: LoF's observer operates in a space devoid of power relations, race, coloniality, or material exclusions. A decolonial diffractive reading [decolonial commitment] would demand that the unmarked state be peopled by those erased by Western epistemic machinery, a dimension LoF cannot accommodate without radical reframing.

## 4. Remaining Gaps & Future Directions

- **GS-LoF-Reentry-Formalization:** The precise formal operation of re-entry in Chapter 12 of *Laws of Form*, especially the 'unwritten cross' and its resolution of the observer/observed hierarchy, remains opaque without primary text extraction. Future cycles must retrieve the primary source and structurally compare it with Varela's calculus of self-reference [S26] and Kauffman's eigenform models.
- **GS-Autopoiesis-Primary-Diffraction:** A systematic diffraction between Maturana/Varela's primary texts (especially *Autopoiesis and Cognition* and *The Tree of Knowledge*) and Spencer-Brown's calculus is missing. Only interview-based parallels (S7) and secondary echoes (S14) are present. Future work should read the biological observer through the formal calculus and vice versa to map the interference pattern of life-as-distinction versus indication-as-life.
- **GS-Material-Discursive-Embodiment:** The diffractive reading with Barad remains theoretical. How would an apparatus that includes sensor noise, glitches, and algorithmic error reconfigure LoF's observer? A material-semiotic embodiment of LoF, perhaps through computational art or new materialist performativity, is necessary to ground the posthumanist observer.
- **GS-Multiplicity-Observers:** The dynamics of multiple observers, conflict, and double contingency (as addressed by Luhmann) are not yet integrated into the diffractive framework. Glanville's self/other and transfer distinction provide a starting point, but a formal calculus of interacting observing systems based on LoF is underdeveloped and requires research into Luhmann's later works and its critics.
- **GS-Decolonial-Recontext:** The Laws of Form observer, despite its radicality, remains enmeshed in a Eurocentric formal tradition. A decolonial diffraction—placing LoF's distinctions alongside indigenous ontologies of relation and the politics of erasure—is absent from the source set and constitutes a necessary future cut.

research_step_results.analyzed_json: {"learnings": ["The observer is implicitly posited as the agent who draws distinctions and makes indications; the axioms begin with 'We take as given the idea of distinction and the idea of indication', embedding the observer in the foundational act.", "Motive is essential: 'There can be no distinction without motive, and there can be no motive unless contents are seen to differ in value' – the observer is a value-perceiving, intentional entity, not a passive recorder.", "The observer's intent is formalized as a signal with a limited use, governed by conventions; the mark is a token whose crossing instruction enacts the observer's operation within the calculus.", "The form of the first distinction constitutes the space in which observer and observed co-emerge; the observer is not external but entangled with the form itself (prefiguring later re-entry)."], "gaps": ["The explicit concept of the observer is not defined or theorized in this excerpt; it remains a presupposition rather than an object of analysis.", "The critical self-referential twist – the observer becoming part of the system through re-entry (introduced later in Laws of Form) – is entirely absent from these initial chapters.", "The relationship between the 'we' who draw distinctions and the algebraic calculus that operates on tokens is not problematized; the observer's material-discursive embodiment and situatedness are erased.", "No mention of how multiple observers or perspectives would interact; the system appears monadic."], "followups": ["Spencer-Brown Laws of Form observer re-entry concept", "Laws of Form chapter 12 observer calculus of indications self-reference", "How does Spencer-Brown define the observer in his notes and commentaries?"], "direct_urls": ["https://randolphdible.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/07/spencer-brown-laws-of-form-ch-1-2-12-and-notes.pdf"], "diffractive_notes": ["The implicit observer aligns with Karen Barad's agential cut: the drawing of a distinction is an intra-action that enacts the ontological separation between observer and observed, rather than reflecting a pre-existing divide. The 'form' is the material-discursive apparatus.", "The law of crossing (recrossing negates crossing) resonates with Deleuze's repetition that produces difference; the observer's repeated act does not return to the same state, akin to the iterative differentiation in becoming.", "From a posthuman/new materialist lens, the unmarked state can be read as the glitch – the void that speaks through algorithmic errors, embodying the apparatus's material agency. The observer's motive becomes a curatorial cut.", "The anti-interactionist commitment: Spencer-Brown's form dissolves the participant/sensory affordance dichotomy; the observer is not an external user interacting with a representation but is folded into the form's material engagement."]}

Published Time: 2005-06-19T14:40:38Z

Markdown Content:
**Second-order cybernetics**, also known as the **cybernetics of cybernetics**, is the recursive application of [cybernetics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cybernetics "Cybernetics") to itself and the reflexive practice of cybernetics according to such a critique. It is cybernetics where "the role of the observer is appreciated and acknowledged rather than disguised, as had become traditional in western science".[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-RG_01-1) Second-order cybernetics was developed between the late 1960s and mid 1970s[[note 1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-4) by [Heinz von Foerster](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Heinz_von_Foerster "Heinz von Foerster") and others, with key inspiration coming from [Margaret Mead](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Margaret_Mead "Margaret Mead"). Foerster referred to it as "the control of control and the communication of communication" and differentiated first-order cybernetics as "the cybernetics of observed systems" and second-order cybernetics as "the cybernetics of observing systems".[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-HVF_01-5)

The concept of second-order cybernetics is closely allied to [radical constructivism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radical_constructivism "Radical constructivism"), which was developed around the same time by [Ernst von Glasersfeld](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ernst_von_Glasersfeld "Ernst von Glasersfeld").[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-RG_RC-6) While it is sometimes considered a break from the earlier concerns of cybernetics, there is much continuity with previous work and it can be thought of as a distinct tradition within cybernetics, with origins in issues evident during the [Macy conferences](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Macy_conferences "Macy conferences") in which cybernetics was initially developed.[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-7)[[7]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-SU_01-8)[[8]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-Brand_01-9) Its concerns include [autonomy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Autonomy "Autonomy"), epistemology, ethics, language, [reflexivity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reflexivity_(social_theory) "Reflexivity (social theory)"), self-consistency, [self-referentiality](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Self-reference "Self-reference"), and [self-organizing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Self-organization "Self-organization") capabilities of [complex systems](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Complex_system "Complex system"), such as in [complexity theory](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Complex_systems_theory "Complex systems theory") (extenuating to the field of [complexity economics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Complexity_economics "Complexity economics")). It has been characterised as cybernetics where "circularity is taken seriously".[[9]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-10)

Second-order cybernetics can be abbreviated as **C2, C 2,** or **SOC**, and is sometimes referred to as the **cybernetics of cybernetics**,[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-MM_01-2)[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-HVF_01-5)[[10]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-HVF_1979-11) or, more rarely, the **new cybernetics**,[[11]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-12)[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-Dupuy-13)[[13]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-GP_01-14)[[14]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-15)[[15]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-16) or **second cybernetics**.[[16]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-17)

These terms are often used interchangeably, but can also stress different aspects:

*   Most specifically, and especially where phrased as the cybernetics of cybernetics, second-order cybernetics is the recursive application of cybernetics to itself. This is closely associated with Mead's 1967 address to the [American Society for Cybernetics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/American_Society_for_Cybernetics "American Society for Cybernetics") (published 1968)[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-MM_01-2) and Foerster's "Cybernetics of Cybernetics"[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-HVF_01-5) book, developed as a course option at the [Biological Computer Laboratory](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biological_Computer_Laboratory "Biological Computer Laboratory") (BCL), where cybernetic texts were analysed according to the principles they put forward. In this sense, second-order cybernetics can be considered the "conscience"[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-RG_01-1) of cybernetics, attending to the subject's consistency and clarity.
*   More generally, second-order cybernetics is the reflexive practice of cybernetics, where cyberneticians understand themselves and other participants to be part of the systems they study and act in, taking a second-order position whether or not it is termed as such. When cybernetics is practiced in this way, second-order cybernetics and cybernetics may be used interchangeably, with the qualifier 'second-order' being used when drawing distinctions from (or critiquing) other approaches (e.g. differentiating from purely technological applications) or as a way of emphasising reflexivity.[[17]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-18)
*   Additionally, and especially where referred to as the new cybernetics, second-order cybernetics may refer to substantial developments in direction and scope taken by cybernetics from the 1970s onward, with greater focus on social and philosophical concerns.

### Initial development

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Second-order_cybernetics&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Initial development")]

Second-order cybernetics took shape during the late 1960s and mid 1970s. The 1967 keynote address to the inaugural meeting of the American Society for Cybernetics (ASC) by Margaret Mead, who had been a participant at the Macy Conferences, is a defining moment in its development. Mead characterised "cybernetics as a way of looking at things and as a language for expressing what one sees",[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-MM_01-2) calling on cyberneticians to assume responsibility for the social consequences of the language of cybernetics and the development of cybernetic systems.[[18]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-KK_01-19) Mead's paper concluded with a proposal directed at the ASC itself, drawing on an earlier suggestion to the Society for General Systems Research (now the [International Society for the Systems Sciences](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/International_Society_for_the_Systems_Sciences "International Society for the Systems Sciences")],[[19]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-20) that it organise itself in the light of the ideas with which it was concerned.[[20]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-21) That is, the practice of cybernetics by the ASC should be subject to cybernetic critique, an idea returned to by [Ranulph Glanville](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ranulph_Glanville "Ranulph Glanville") in his time as president of the society.[[21]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-RG_02-22)[[22]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-23)

Mead's paper was published in 1968 in a collection edited by Heinz von Foerster.[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-MM_01-2) With Mead uncontactable due to field work at the time, Foerster titled the paper "Cybernetics of Cybernetics", a title that perhaps emphasised his concerns more than Mead's.[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-RG_01-1) Foerster promoted second-order cybernetics energetically, developing it as a means of renewal for cybernetics generally and as what has been called an "unfinished revolution" in science.[[23]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-BCL-24) Foerster developed second-order cybernetics as a critique of realism and objectivity and as a radically reflexive form of science, where observers enter their domains of observation, describing their own observing not the supposed causes.

The initial development of second-order cybernetics was consolidated by the mid 1970s in a series of significant developments and publications. These included: the 1974 publication of the "Cybernetics of Cybernetics" book, edited by Foerster,[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-HVF_01-5)[[note 2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-25) developed as a course option at the BCL examining various texts from cybernetics according to the principles they proposed;[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-HVF_01-5)[autopoiesis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Autopoiesis "Autopoiesis"), developed by biologists [Humberto Maturana](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Humberto_Maturana "Humberto Maturana") and [Francisco Varela](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Francisco_Varela "Francisco Varela");[[24]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-26)[[25]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-27)[conversation theory](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Conversation_theory "Conversation theory"), developed by Gordon Pask, Bernard Scott and Dionysius Kallikourdis;[[26]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-28)[[27]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-29)[[28]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-30)[[29]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-31)[[30]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-32)[[3]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-Pask_CT-3)[radical constructivism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radical_constructivism "Radical constructivism"), developed by [Ernst von Glasersfeld](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ernst_von_Glasersfeld "Ernst von Glasersfeld");[[31]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-33) and other explorations of self-reference, including Foerster's eigen-forms[[32]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-F261-34) and Glanville's theory of objects.[[33]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-35)

### Participant observers

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Second-order_cybernetics&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Participant observers")]

A key concept in second-order cybernetics is that observers (and other actors, such as designers, modellers, users...) are to be understood as participants within the systems with which they are engaged, in contrast to the detachment implied in objectivity and conventional scientific practice. This includes cyberneticians inclusion of themselves in the practice of cybernetics, as well as the inclusion of participants within the consideration and design of systems more generally.

Second-order cybernetics' emphasis on participation and inclusion has led to affinities and overlaps with [action research](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Action_research "Action research"),[[7]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-SU_01-8) design,[[34]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-RG_Try_again-36)[[35]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-BS_01-37) and the creative arts.[[36]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-TS_01-38)

While second-order cybernetics continues to use of the terms observing and observers following Foerster's formulation, [Ranulph Glanville](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ranulph_Glanville "Ranulph Glanville") has suggested using the terms composition and composers instead to better indicate the active role of participation.[[37]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-39)

### Ethical implications

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Second-order_cybernetics&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Ethical implications")]

The critique of objectivity developed in second-order cybernetics has led to a concern with ethical issues. Foerster developed a critique of morality in ethical terms, arguing for ethics to remain implicit in action.[[38]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-HVF_Ethics-40) Foerster's position has been described as an "ethics of enabling ethics"[[39]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-41) or as a form of "recursive ethical questioning".[[40]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-42) Varela published a short book on "ethical know-how".[[41]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-43) Glanville identified a number of "desirable" ethical qualities implicit in the cybernetic devices of the black box, distinction, autonomy, and [conversation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Conversation_theory "Conversation theory").[[42]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-44) Others have drawn connections to design[[43]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-45)[[44]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-46) and critical systems heuristics.[[45]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-47)

### Relationship to first-order cybernetics

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Second-order_cybernetics&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Relationship to first-order cybernetics")]

[![Image 1](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/SOCyberntics.png/330px-SOCyberntics.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:SOCyberntics.png)

Diagram from [Stewart Brand](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stewart_Brand "Stewart Brand")'s 1976 conversation with Margaret Mead and Gregory Bateson, noting that they and Norbert Wiener understood themselves as participant observers in contrast to the detached "input-output" approach typical of engineering

The relationship of first-order and second-order cybernetics can be compared to that between [Isaac Newton](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isaac_Newton "Isaac Newton")'s view of the universe and that of [Albert Einstein](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Albert_Einstein "Albert Einstein").[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-RG_01-1) Just as Newton's description remains appropriate and usable in many circumstances, even flights to the moon, so first-order cybernetics also provides everything that is needed in many circumstances. In the same way that the Newtonian view is understood to be a special, restricted version of Einstein's view, so first-order cybernetics may be understood as a special, restricted version of second-order cybernetics.

The distinction between first and second-order cybernetics is sometimes used as a form of periodisation, while can obscure the continuity between earlier and later cybernetics,[[note 3]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-49)[[35]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-BS_01-37) with what would come to be called second-order qualities evident in the work of cyberneticians such as [Warren McCulloch](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_McCulloch "Warren McCulloch")[[7]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-SU_01-8) and Gregory Bateson,[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-RG_01-1) and with Foerster and Mead being both Macy conference participants and instigators of second-order cybernetics. Mead and Bateson, for instance, noted that they and Wiener understood themselves as participant observers in contrast to the detached "input-output" approach typical of engineering.[[8]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-Brand_01-9) In this sense, second-order cybernetics can be thought of as a distinct tradition within cybernetics that developed along different lines to the narrower framing of [engineering cybernetics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Engineering_cybernetics "Engineering cybernetics").[[7]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-SU_01-8)

Pask summarized the differences between the old and the new cybernetics as a shift in emphasis:[[47]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-AC_03-50)[[48]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-51) from information to coupling, from the reproduction of "order-from-order" ([Schroedinger](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Erwin_Schr%C3%B6dinger "Erwin Schrödinger") 1944) to the generation of "order-from-noise" ([von Foerster](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Heinz_von_Foerster "Heinz von Foerster") 1960), from transmission of data to conversation, and from external observation to participant observation.

### Third and higher orders

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Second-order_cybernetics&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Third and higher orders")]

Some see the definition of third and higher orders of cybernetics as a next step in the development of the discipline, but this has not won widespread acceptance. Attempts to define a third order of cybernetics have been concerned with embedding the participant observer of second-order cybernetics explicitly within broader social and/or ecological[[49]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-52) contexts.

Foerster discouraged the definition of higher orders, regarding the distinction between first-and second as an either/or regarding the position of the cyberneticians with regard to their system of concern.[[50]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFoerster2003301-53)

## Heinz von Foerster and the Biological Computer Laboratory

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Second-order_cybernetics&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Heinz von Foerster and the Biological Computer Laboratory")]

Second-order cybernetics is closely identified with Heinz von Foerster and the work of the Biological Computer Laboratory (BCL) at the University of Illinois Urbana–Champaign.

Foerster attributes the origin of second-order cybernetics to the attempts by cyberneticians to construct a model of the mind:

> ... a brain is required to write a theory of a brain. From this follows that a theory of the brain, that has any aspirations for completeness, has to account for the writing of this theory. And even more fascinating, the writer of this theory has to account for her or himself. Translated into the domain of cybernetics; the cybernetician, by entering his own domain, has to account for his or her own activity. Cybernetics then becomes cybernetics of cybernetics, or _second-order cybernetics_.[[51]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second-order_cybernetics#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFoerster2003289-54)

> Journal for the Theory of Social Behaviour 31:3 0021–8308 © The Executive Management Committee/Blackwell Publishers Ltd. 2001. Published by Blackwell Publishers 108 Cowley Road, Oxford, OX4 1JF UK and 350 Main Street, Malden, MA 02148, USA.

# Observing Systems: a Cybernetic Perspective on System/Environment Relations RAF VANDERSTRAETEN Descriptions of society have changed during the preceding decades. Expressions such as “knowledge society” or “information society” are nowadays generally used. In science, and social science in particular, the concept of information has entered as fundamental concept. It is no longer relegated to special fields of inquiry, such as public opinion research, mass communication or educational technology (cf. Craig, 1999). Instead, this concept, and the closely related con-cepts of communication and knowledge, are replacing the fundamental analytical categories of social action, social exchange, social role, etc. As is well-known, the entry of these concepts into the foundations of science after World War II coincided with the diffusion of new technologies of information processing. It has been convincingly demonstrated that there is a relatively direct lineage from the early cybernetic information theories to the adoption of constructivist theories of information and communication in contemporary social theory (Hayles, 1999). In this paper, I want to argue that the potential of cybernetics has not yet been fully utilized. A careful (re)consideration of the insights of cybernetics opens up new horizons for social theory. The first sections of this paper will focus on the rise of second-order cybernetics and its constructivist epistemology. Special attention is paid to the epistemological consequences of the mathematical cal-culus of George Spencer Brown. Afterwards, I will highlight its impact on the writings of the sociologist Niklas Luhmann. In the final section, I argue for a redefinition of social rationality that incorporates the insights of second-order cybernetics. 1. AUTOPOIESIS AND COGNITION Cybernetics is a relatively young scientific discipline. The publication of Norbert Wiener’s Cybernetics in 1948 is often identified as the official start of the discipline. The subtitle of Wiener’s book, Control and communication in the animal and 298 Raf Vanderstraeten 

> © The Executive Management Committee/Blackwell Publishers Ltd. 2001

the machine , at the same time highlighted the subject matter of cybernetics, viz. regulatory processes in living organisms and thinking machines. The discipline focused on the formalization and modeling of information processing within systems-plus-environments. The paradigmatic artifact of the young discipline was the homeostat. The homeostat was an electric device constructed with transducers and variable resistors. When it received an input changing its state, it searched for the con-

fi guration of variables that would return it to its initial condition. This homeostat was meant to model a system that must keep essential variables within preset limits to survive. From this cybernetic perspective, homeostasis indicated a universally desirable state, a requirement “uniform between the inanimate and the anim-ate ” (Ashby, 1953, p. 73; cf. 1956, pp. 195 –201). Departure from homeostasis would threaten death for every living organism. The basic assumption was that a living organism is a mechanism that responds to its environment by trying to maintain its homeostatic stability or equilibrium. This perspective attracted a lot of attention in the 1950s and 1960s, also among prominent social scientists (e.g. Claude L évi-Strauss, Talcott Parsons). However, the assumptions encoded in the cybernetic artifacts did not go unques-tioned. In the fi eld of cybernetics itself, the epistemological status of the mechan-istic models displaying input, output and feedback loops was put into question. How does information circulate between the mechanistic models and the people who make those models? As Gregory Bateson argued, the observer needs to be made part of the picture when studying the “cybernetic nature of self and the world ” (M.C. Bateson, 1972, p. 16). In this “cybernetic epistemology, ” realist assumptions are replaced by constructivist assumptions. Following Gregory and Catherine Bateson, our knowledge is the end result of the internal processes we use to construct our inner world (perception, cognition). The inner world is a metaphor for the outer world. “Each person is his own central metaphor ” (M.C. Bateson, 1972, p. 285). In an attempt to avoid the pitfall of solipsism, the Batesons also accorded an important role to objective constraints, for they insisted that only those constructions that are compatible with reality are conducive to long-term survival. Gregory Bateson ’s objections to classical cybernetics were provoked by cyber-netic research on information processing in living organisms. In breaking new ground, Bateson drew on a famous article on the frog ’s visual cortex, “What the frog ’s eye tells the frog ’s brain. ” Its authors had implanted microelectrodes in a frog ’s visual cortex to measure the strengths of neural responses to various stimuli. They registered that small objects in fast, erratic motion elicited maximum response, whereas large, slow-moving objects evoked little or no response. The frog ’s perceptual equipment allows it to perceive fl ies while ignoring other phe-nomena irrelevant to its interests. These results imply that the frog ’s perceptual system does not so much register reality as construct it. As the authors noted, while generalizing the results of their work, “it shows that the eye speaks to the brain Observing Systems 299 © The Executive Management Committee/Blackwell Publishers Ltd. 2001 in a language already highly organized and interpreted, instead of transmitting some more or less accurate copy of the distribution of lights upon the receptors ”

(Lettvin, Maturana, McCulloch & Pitts, 1965, p. 251). In a recent discussion of these developments, Hayles aptly remarked: “No wonder that the article was quickly recognized as a classic, for it blew a frog-sized hole in realist epistemology ”

(1999, p. 135). At the end of the 1960s, the fundamental problem for cybernetics was how to rede fi ne homeostatic systems so that the observer could be taken into account. The breakthrough came with work that led to the publication of Autopoiesis and Cognition (Maturana & Varela, 1980). Humberto Maturana —also one of the co-authors of the article on the frog ’s visual cortex —arrived at his insights, he explains in the book ’s introduction, by deciding to treat “the activity of the nervous system as determined by the nervous system itself, and not by the external world ” (1980, p. xv). His key insight was to realize that if the action of the nervous system is determined by its organization, the result is a circular dynamic. A living system ’s organization causes certain products to be produced (for example, nucleic acids). These products in turn produce the organization characteristic of that living system. To describe this circularity, he coined the term autopoiesis or self-production. A living system, then, responds to its environ-ment in ways determined by its autopoiesis. It constructs its environment through the domain of interactions made possible by its autopoietic organization. A living system operates within the boundaries of an organization that closes in on itself and leaves the world on the outside. The central premise of Autopoiesis and Cognition —that systems are informationally closed —radically alters the idea of the cybernetic informational feedback loop, for the loop no longer functions to connect a system to its environment. In the autopoietic perspective, no information crosses the boundary separating the system from its environment. We do not see a world “out there ” that exists apart from us. Rather, we see only what our systemic organization allows us to see (see also Maturana & Varela, 1988; Maturana, 1997). The world merely irritates; it triggers changes determined by the system ’s own organization. This is expressed by the formula “order from noise. ” Only a closed system is able to know (the world). Moreover, “everything said is said by an observer ” (Maturana & Varela, 1980, p. xxii; cf. von Foerster, 1978; Vanderstraeten, 1997). But how do observing systems observe? In second-order cybernetics, one draws —conform to the discipline ’s background —on a mathematical calculus to clarify the basic operations of observing systems: George Spencer Brown ’s Laws of Form (1971) . “The fundamental cognitive operation that an observer performs is the operation of distinction ” (Maturana & Varela, 1980, p. xxii; see also Varela, 1979). Accordingly, the construction of the world depends upon the processing of distinctions (or, in Spencer Brown ’s terminology, on forms). When something is observed, it is indicated as being distinct from something else. The indication of one side of the distinction provides the system with information of the kind of: 300 Raf Vanderstraeten 

> © The Executive Management Committee/Blackwell Publishers Ltd. 2001

this-and-not-that, this-and-not-something-else. The distinction divides the world into what is momentarily included and what is excluded. Following second-order cybernetics, it is the system ’s distinction that de fi nes the meaning of what is observed. This implies that one needs to observe the system and the distinctions that it uses in order to analyze this system ’s universe. One needs to observe the observer and not the objective world. As Spencer Brown remarks: “Our under-standing of such an universe comes not from discovering its present appearance, but in remembering what we originally did to bring it about ” (1971, p. 104). An example may clarify the background of this theoretical move. If a system intends to observe and describe processes of variability or change within its environment, it needs to use terms and sentences that cannot participate in, nor represent this variability. The terms and sentences it uses have to be interpreted to add the experience of variability. “When described, the variability is repres-ented by constancy, namely by the time-independent describing sentences. It is when we interpret the sentences that we add the reality, the described variabil-ity ” (L öfgren, 1981, p. 130). But if changes are described by means of constants, these constants contradict the (description of the) variability itself. An observing system cannot produce internal correlates of the dynamics of its environment. It can only orient itself to itself. It has to fi nd its way by its own guidance. An observing system can only construct its own distinctions, such as fast/slow, vari-able/constant, before/after. It is, in Gregory Bateson ’s words, “a difference which makes a difference ” (1972, p. 453). This way, cybernetics starts a re fl ection upon the role of the observer in the observed universe. It abandons the subject/object distinction of traditional epistemologies and tries to include itself (as observer) among its objects (what is observed). It focuses on a description of the describer, on an observation of the observer, on a cybernetics of cybernetics. Cybernetics becomes second-order cybernetics. This switch is particularly clearly brought to the fore in the in fl u-ential collection of essays by Heinz von Foerster, Observing Systems (1984). As the punning title already recognizes, the observer of systems can himself be consti-tuted as a system to be observed. Before discussing the implications of second-order cybernetics, especially with regard to the study of ecological communication in society, I will review in greater detail Spencer Brown ’s differential logic (cf. Baecker, 1993, 1999). 2. LAWS OF FORM In an extremely formal way, Spencer Brown de fi nes observing as the application of a distinction. A distinction or form has two sides. It consists of a boundary that makes it possible to distinguish and separate both sides. The separation of the two sides makes it necessary to depart from one side of the distinction (the 

“marked state ” or “inner space ”), and not from the other (the “unmarked state ”Observing Systems 301 © The Executive Management Committee/Blackwell Publishers Ltd. 2001 or “outer space ”). The observation must indicate what is being observed; a reference has to be given. Herein lies at the same time the hidden indication that there remains the other side which is (for the moment at least) not mentioned. If one thus equates observing with distinguishing, it becomes clear that the choice of a distinction entails important consequences. For example, the old tradition that distinguishes between humans and animals, and between animals and humans, has oriented both zoology and humanism towards a particular outlook on the characteristics of perfection, on de fi cits, on positions within the Creation, etc. The choice of a distinction is not an innocent event. It is already, as French philosophers would say, an act of violence (e.g. Derrida, 1979, pp. 117 –228). 1This starting point is yet relatively simple —but it is built upon a complex foundation. Following Spencer Brown, an observation is an operation that applies a distinction to indicate one side of the distinction, and not the other. It encompasses two entwined components: distinguish-and-indicate. During the observation itself, these components cannot be separated nor blended. “We take as given the idea of distinction and the idea of indication, and that we cannot make an indication without drawing a distinction ” (Spencer Brown, 1971, p. 1). This starting point raises the question about the position of the distinction itself. If the distinction makes it possible to indicate one of its sides, the distinction cannot contain itself. Every distinction has two sides, but is itself neither found on one, nor on the other side. The distinction itself remains unobservable , because it cannot be indicated as one of the sides of the distinction (Varela, 1975). It is, of course, possible to distinguish distinctions. One can call a book expens-ive (and not cheap), new (and not used), slim (and not thick), exciting (and not boring), etc. But this distinction of distinctions does not solve but only postpones the problem. In this case, the distinction between distinctions cannot be found in itself, etc. Every distinction is a “blind spot, ” and this blindness is presupposed as the condition of the possibility of the observation. One can also say that an observation is inevitably uncritical with regard to its own reference. According to Spencer Brown ’s account, the observation is at the same time a simple and a compound operation. It is a twoness which is an oneness: distinguish-and-indicate. It rests on the distinction between distinction and indication. This implies that one cannot start with making a distinction without already having made one. The fi rst distinction is and is not the fi rst distinction (Glanville & Varela, 1981; Kauffman, 1987). This paradox, however, does not paralyze Spencer Brown ’s calculus. The calculus starts with a simple directive which ignores the problem: “Draw a distinction ” (1971, p. 3). The discussion of the para-dox is postponed, until the calculus is suf fi ciently complex to introduce the fi gure of a “re-entry ” of the distinction into the inner space, of the form into the form (1971, pp. 56 –57, pp. 69 –76; cf. Esposito, 1996). Thus, the calculus is able to account for circular or self-referential processes. It can re fl ect onto itself; it can observe its own observations (e.g. observe the distinction between distinction and indication). The calculus ‘survives ’ its basic paradox, although it cannot eliminate 302 Raf Vanderstraeten 

> © The Executive Management Committee/Blackwell Publishers Ltd. 2001

it. It unfolds its paradox. More precisely, its analysis of observations and descrip-tions ensues from the analysis of the problems of self-observation and self-description .What gets excluded, from this point of view, is an universe which is entirely accessible to itself. In order to see itself, the world “must fi rst cut itself up into at least one state which sees, and at least one other state which is seen. In this severed and mutilated condition, whatever it sees is only partially itself. We may take it that the world undoubtedly is itself (i.e. is indistinct from itself ), but, in any attempt to see itself as an object, it must, equally undoubtedly, act so as to make itself distinct from, and therefore false to, itself ” (Spencer Brown, 1971, p. 105). What gets excluded is an observation which is not constructed. The calculus cannot establish an outside perspective, an Archimedean position. But it allows for the calculation of the implications of particular distinctions via the operation of “re-entry. ” The operation of “re-entry ” indicates the possibility of reintroduc-ing the distinction which a system uses into its inner space, thus the possibility of directing the system ’s information processing by means of the unity of the distinction 

which it uses. To this point, I will return in the fi nal section of this article. 3. OBSERVING SYSTEMS Following second-order cybernetics, the world cannot be objectively represented within the system. It cannot instruct an observing system. The world is con-structed. “The environment contains no information; the environment is as it is ”

(von Foerster, 1984, p. 263). The world is virtual information; only an observing system produces factual information. Every bit of information is an internal construct (von Foerster, 1999). I will indicate some important results of second-order cybernetics that bear upon ongoing epistemological debates, and then pro-ceed to a discussion of the dominant modes of observing within contemporary society. (a) Debates about the merits or defects of constructivism have hitherto pre-dominantly focused on issues of construction versus representation (cf. von Glasersfeld, 1995; Gergen, 1998; Hacking, 1998, 1999). In different brands of constructivism, it is argued that reality is a construction and that this construction does not correspond with the external world. But what does this “not ” mean? Why are observing systems not instructed by the world? Cybernetics provides a clear answer to these questions. It is the distinction between the marked state (what is indicated) and the unmarked state (what is not indicated) which is imagined by the observing system. An observ-ing system explores which constructions or distinctions can be inscribed on the world. The contribution of the observing system, which allows for the construction of information, consists in the act of distinguishing. In other words: observations cannot copy, depict or represent an external reality Observing Systems 303 © The Executive Management Committee/Blackwell Publishers Ltd. 2001 inside an observing system, because it is not possible to observe a reality that exists independent of the observation (that is, independent of the distinction drawn by the observing system). 2 The world “as it really is ” is located in the unobservable “unmarked space ” (Spencer Brown). In this light, the Batesons ’ idea of a metaphorical representation of the external world also needs to be given up. (b) Constructivism rede fi nes the concept of “resistance, ” which is traditionally used to underpin the referential capacity of knowledge. For Aristotle, ta physika are characterized by the resistance they oppose to us and they thus become objects of our cognition. It is, accordingly, by virtue of th
research_step_results.analyzed_json: {"learnings": ["Second-order cybernetics displaces the observer from an external, detached position to an integral component of the system, making observation a constitutive operation within the system/environment distinction.", "Spencer-Brown's Laws of Form formalizes observation as the simultaneous act of drawing a distinction and indicating one of its sides—an operation that inevitably produces an unmarked state (the blind spot of the observer).", "Luhmann radicalizes Spencer-Brown by applying the calculus to social systems: an observer is not a subject but the system that produces itself through its own operations of distinction and indication, thereby absorbing the observer into the observed (re-entry).", "Second-order observation observes the blind spot of first-order observers, revealing how every observation is paradoxical (it cannot observe its own distinction) and thus forecloses any final, objective reality."], "gaps": ["The text does not explicate the formal mechanics of Spencer-Brown's calculus—the primary arithmetic and algebra of the mark, the law of calling, the law of crossing, and the operation of re-entry—only their epistemological consequences.", "A diffractive comparison between Spencer-Brown's observer and the observer in other second-order cybernetic frameworks (e.g., von Foerster's eigen-behaviors, Maturana and Varela's autopoietic observer) is absent.", "The material-semiotic dimension of the observer as an intra-acting apparatus (in the Baradian sense) is not explored; the discussion remains at the level of formal and sociological abstraction without engaging the physical or computational substrate."], "followups": ["https://cepa.info/fulltexts/1133.pdf (full text for rigorous reading of Spencer-Brown's calculus in the context of Luhmann's sociology)", "What is the precise formal operation of re-entry in Laws of Form and how does it dissolve the hierarchy between observer and observed?", "How does the Spencer-Brownian observer differ from the autopoietic observer in Maturana and Varela's biology of cognition?"], "direct_urls": [], "diffractive_notes": ["The observer's unmarked state—the blind spot that cannot be indicated—resonates with the core belief in the glitch, sensor noise, and algorithmic error as the voice of the apparatus. The entanglement of observer and observed collapses the interactionist dichotomy, aligning with the anti-interactionist commitment to entanglement over seamless affordance.", "The notion that 'each person is his own central metaphor' (M.C.
